6.9 The data in Table 6.10, taken from Solomon (1961), lists the
responses, (agree/disagree), given by 2982 New Jersey high-school
seniors in a 1957 attitude survey, in response to the following four
propositions:

1\. The development of new ideas is the scientist's greatest source
of satisfaction.

2\. Scientists and engineers should be eliminated from the military
draft.

3\. The scientist will make his maximum contribution to society
when he has freedom to work on problems that interest him.

4\. The monetary compensation of a Nobel Prize-winner in physics
should be at least equal to that given to popular entertainers.

Examine how each response marginally depends on the I.Q.
group.

Examine the six bivariate distributions to see whether the odds-
ratios are different in the two groups.

Give a brief summary of your conclusions in non-technical
language.

6.10 Show that the redundancy in the transformation following
(6.10) can be avoided by eliminating all components having an

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="240" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOG-LINEAR MODELS" -->

index whose value is 1.

6.11 Show that for a bivariate response $\left( A , B \right) ,$ in which $A$ is a
nominal response with three levels and $B$ is ordinal with four levels,
the natural analogue of the bivariate logit transformation is

$$\eta _ { a i } = \log \left( \gamma _ { i _ { \bullet } } / \gamma _ { 3 _ { * } } \right) \quad i = 1 , 2$$
$$\eta _ { b j } = \log \left\{ \gamma _ { \cdot j } / \left( 1 - \gamma _ { \cdot j } \right) \right\} \quad j = 1 , 2 , 3$$
$$\eta _ { a b i j } = \log \left( \frac { \gamma _ { i j } } { \gamma _ { i . } - \gamma _ { i j } } \right) - \log \left( \frac { \gamma _ { 3 j } } { \gamma _ { 3 . } - \gamma _ { 3 j } } \right)$$
$$= \log \mathrm { i t } \left( \gamma _ { i j } / \gamma _ { i . } \right) - \mathrm { l o g i t } \left( \gamma _ { 3 j } / \gamma _ { 3 . } \right) .$$

In these expressions $\gamma _ { i j }$ is defined as

$$\gamma _ { i j } = \mathrm { p r } \left( A = i , B \leq j \right)$$
$$\gamma _ { i _ { * } } = \mathrm { p r } \left( A = i \right)$$
$$\gamma _ { \cdot j } = \mathrm { p r } \left( B \leq j \right) .$$

6.12 The data listed in Table 6.11, taken from Diaconis (1988),
give partial results for the 1980 American Psychological Association
presidential election in which there were five candidates, here
labelled $A , B , C , D$ and E. For each of the 120 complete rankings
of the five candidates, Table 6.11 gives the number of voters who
cast their ballots in that way. Thus of the 5738 voters who cast
complete ballots, the modal group of 186 voters cast their ballots as
'23154' in which candidate $A$ was placed second, candidate B third,
candidate $\mathrm { C }$ first and so on. Incomplete ballots are not included
here.

1\. Create five factors, $A ,$ B,C, $D ,$ $E ,$ each with five levels, such
that $A$ at level 4 means that candidate $A$ was ranked in fourth
position, and so on for the remaining factors. Fit the log-linear
models 1 and $A + B + C + D + E .$ What is the rank of the
latter model matrix. Explain why $E$ can be dropped without
affecting the fit.

2\. Create linear and quadratic contrasts for each of the five
factors such that $B _ { L }$ takes values -2, -1,0, 1, 2 and $B _ { Q }$ takes
values 2, -1, -2, -1, 2 for the five levels of $B .$ Compute the
sum of the model vectors $A _ { L } + B _ { L } + C _ { L } + D _ { L } + E _ { L } .$ Fit the
model

$$A _ { L } + B _ { L } + C _ { L } + D _ { L } + E _ { L } .$$

Which candidate has the smallest coefficient? Interpret the
sizes of these coefficients.

3\. Add the terms

$$A _ { Q } + B _ { Q } + C _ { Q } + D _ { Q } + E _ { Q }$$

to the previous model. Which candidate has the largest quad-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="242" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LOG-LINEAR MODELS" -->

ratic coefficient? Interpret the sizes of the quadratic coeffi-
cients in terms of heterogeneity among voters and negative
voting. Examine the two-way table of total votes indexed by
candidate and position. Compute the fitted values for this
table under the quadratic model just fitted.

Show that $E _ { L }$ and $E _ { Q }$ can be dropped from the model
without affecting the fit.

4\. Create all 10 $\mathrm { l i n e a r } \times \mathrm { l i n e a r }$ interaction contrasts of the type
$A _ { L } B _ { L } = A _ { L } \times B _ { L } .$ Add these to the previous model. Show
that the fit is substantially improved, even though the resid-
ual deviance is still considerably larger than the degrees of
freedom. Interpret the coefficients obtained.

5\. What additional systematic effects might be present here?
State these effects qualitatively. How would you incorporate
these into your model?

6\. Which candidate has the most first-place votes? Which candi-
date is least disliked? Which candidate ought to be declared
the winner?

7\. What modifications of this procedure would you use to analyse
the incomplete ballots in which three or fewer candidates are
ranked by some voters?

8\. Negative voting can be accomplished effectively only with
a complete ballot. What implications does this have for
comparisons of complete ballots with incomplete ballots?

6.13 Let $A B$ be a two-level factor taking the level 1 if $A$ precedes
$B$ in the permutation, and level 2 otherwise. Nine other factors
$A C , \ldots , D E$ are defined likewise. Thus the model matrix $X$
corresponding to the model formula

$$1 + A B + A C + A D + A E + B C + B D + B E + C D + C E + D E$$

is the incidence matrix for the set of inversions required to trans-
form $\pi$ to standard order. What is the rank of $\mathrm { X }$ Fit this model
to the data in Table 6.11.

By extension, let $A B C$ be a six-level factor, one level for each
of the possible orders of $A ,$ $B ,$ $C$ in the permutation $\pi .$ Nine other
factors $A B D , \ldots , C D E$ are defined in like manner. Show that $A B ,$
$A C$ and $B C$ are marginal to $A B C .$ What is the rank of the model

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="243" -->
<!-- PageHeader="6.8 EXERCISES 6" -->

matrix $Z$ corresponding to the model formula

$$1 + A B C + A B D + A B E + A C D + A C E$$
$$+ A D E + B C D + B C E + B D E + C D E ?$$

Show that the column space of $X$ is included in the column space
of $Z$ and that

$$\mathrm { r a n k } \left( Z \right) = 1 + \left( \begin{array}{} { k } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right) + 2 \left( \begin{array}{} { k } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right) ,$$

where $k$ is the number of candidates.

Fit the model $Z$ to the data in Table 6.11. Give a substantive
explanation for the improvement in fit. Show that these models
are unaffected by re-labelling candidates. [Babington-Smith, 1950;
Mallows, 1957].

6.14 Use the data in Table 4.10 to test the hypothesis that mating
occurs at random, at least as regards eye-colour. Take $Y$ to be the
$6 \times 1$ vector of counts for the various eye-colour combinations of
the parents. Formulate the hypothesis of random mating as a log-
linear model for $Y$ as response. State what assumptions you have
made and indicate whether these assumptions are reasonable in this
context. Fit the model and use it to estimate the proportions of
light-eyed, hazel-eyed and dark-eyed individuals in the population.

6.15 The butler effect: It can safely be assumed in Table 4.10 that
a small proportion $\epsilon$ of the children are not the biological children
of the putative fathers. Consider how you might estimate $\epsilon$ under
the following assumptions:

1\. If both biological parents are light-eyed the children are in-
variably light-eyed.

2\. The distribution of eye-colour is the same for both sexes.

3\. The population of butlers is comparable, at least as regards
eye-colour, to the population of putative fathers.

4\. There are no recording errors in the data.

Other assumptions that might be reasonable include the fol-
lowing: (a) If both biological parents are dark-eyed, the children
are light-eyed with probability $1 / 4 .$ (b) If one parent is light-eyed
and one dark-eyed the children are light-eyed with probability $1 / 2 .$
Consider how these additional assumptions might be used to im-
prove the estimate of $\epsilon .$

6.16 In his 1898 monograph $\mathrm { L } .$ von Bortkewitsch gives the now
famous record of deaths by horse-kicks of soldiers in the Prussian
army from 1875 to 1894. The data for $c = 1 4$ army corps over
$r = 2 0$ years are given by Andrews and Herz berg (1985, pp.17-18).
Fit the log-linear model $\mathrm { c o r p s } + \mathrm { y e a r } .$ Is the fit adequate? The
data are sparse, which suggests that the usual $x ^ { 2 }$ approximation
☒
may not be accurate.

The Haldane-Dawson formulae (Haldane, 1939; Dawson, 1954)
for the exact mean and variance of $X ^ { 2 }$ for the model of indepen-
☒
dence in a two-way table are

$$E \left( X ^ { 2 } \right) = \left( r - 1 \right) \left( c - 1 \right) N / \left( N - 1 \right) ,$$
$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( X ^ { 2 } \right) = 2 N \left( \nu - \sigma \right) \left( \mu - \tau \right) / \left( N - 3 \right) + N ^ { 2 } \sigma \tau / \left( N - 1 \right) ,$$ ☒ ☒

where

$$\nu = \left( N - r \right) \left( r - 1 \right) / \left( N - 1 \right) , \quad \sigma = N \left\{ \sum _ { i } s _ { i } ^ { - 1 } - r ^ { 2 } / N \right\} / \left( N - 2 \right) ,$$
$$\mu = \left( N - c \right) \left( c - 1 \right) / \left( N - 1 \right) , \quad \tau = N \left\{ \sum _ { j } t _ { j } ^ { - 1 } - c ^ { 2 } / N \right\} / \left( N - 2 \right) ,$$

The row and column totals are $s _ { i } ,$ $t _ { j } ,$ and $N = \sum s _ { i } = \sum t _ { j } .$

Use these formulae for the horse-kick data to show that the mean
and variance of $X ^ { 2 }$ are 248.27 and 419.81 respectively. What is the
☒

variance of the usual $x ^ { 2 }$ approximation?
☒

Is there any evidence of variation in the accident rate over years
or between corps? For further details see Quine and Seneta (1987)
or Preece, Ross and Kirby (1988).

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 7 Conditional likelihoods


### 7.1 Introduction

In many applications the likelihood function involves several par-
ameters, only a few of which are of interest to the investigator.
The remaining parameters, often pejoratively called incidental or
nuisance parameters, are necessary in order that the model make
sense physically, but their values are largely irrelevant to the exper-
iment and to the conclusions that are to be drawn. To take a simple
example, consider a comparative experiment with two treatment
groups in which the response is ordinal with $k$ categories. The
proportional-odds model,

$$\log \left\{ \gamma _ { i j } / \left( 1 - \gamma _ { i j } \right) \right\} = \theta _ { j } - \beta x _ { i } , \quad j = 1 , \ldots , k - 1 ; i = 1 , 2 ,$$

in which $x _ { i }$ is an indicator variable for treatment group, involves
one parameter of interest, $\beta ,$ and $k \quad - \quad 1$ nuisance parameters,
$\theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { k - 1 } .$ There are applications in which both components
$\theta$ and $\beta$ are of equal interest to the investigator, but usually, in
comparative experiments, the focus is on change or rate of change
of the response as the stimulus is increased. Thus, when we use
the terms 'nuisance parameter' or 'incidental parameter' it is with
certain common applications in mind. The status of a parameter
depends on the context.

Two difficulties arise in dealing with likelihood functions that
depend on a large number of incidental parameters in addition to
the effects of interest. First, from a purely mathematical point of
view, there is no guarantee of consistency or optimality in the limit
as the number of parameters increases in proportion to the data

*This chapter contains more mathematical material and may be omit-
ted on first reading.

<!-- PageNumber="245" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="246" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

accumulated. Whether this large-sample mathematical difficulty
has any relevance in the finite samples actually observed is another
matter, and will not be discussed here. The second difficulty is the
purely numerical one of maximizing a function of many variables
and of obtaining the inverse matrix of second derivatives, but this is
a subsidiary consideration in view of the first difficulty. For these
reasons, we seek a modified likelihood function that depends on
as few of the incidental parameters as possible while, at the same
time, sacrificing as little information as possible. Inferences are
then based on this modified likelihood function, particularly on its
shape in the vicinity of its maximum.


### 7.2 Marginal and conditional likelihoods


#### 7.2.1 Marginal likelihood

One way of eliminating unwanted nuisance parameters is to work
with the marginal likelihood for a suitably chosen subset of the
complete data vector. This method does not always work satisfac-
torily, but when it does, it is clearly desirable to choose as large a
subset of the original data as possible so that the information loss
is minimized.

In the context of the bivariate logistic model (6.25), if $\beta ^ { \left( a \right) }$ is the
parameter of interest and $\left( \beta ^ { \left( b \right) } , \beta ^ { \left( a b \right) } \right)$ are nuisance parameters, we
may eliminate the nuisance parameters by working with the log
likelihood for the marginal variable $A$ alone, i.e. with the marginal
totals $\left( Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } \right) .$ Evidently, from the analysis in section 6.6.1, some
small loss of information is thereby incurred, but this loss might
well be judged acceptable in view of the simplicity achieved. In this
example, the loss of efficiency is balanced by a gain in robustness,
because the marginal likelihood estimates are consistent whether or
not the assumed models for $\eta _ { a }$ and $\eta _ { a b }$ in (6.19) are correct, whereas
the estimates derived from the full likelihood are not protected
against such mis-specifications.

To take an unrelated example, suppose $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are observa-
tions taken at spatial locations $s _ { 1 } , \ldots , s _ { n } ,$ and that the n-vector $\mathrm { Y }$
has the multivariate Normal distribution with cumulants

$$E \left( Y \right) = X \beta ; \quad \text { cov } \left( Y \right) = \Sigma \left( \theta \right) ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="247" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.2 MARGINAL AND CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

where $\Sigma \left( \theta \right)$ is a known covariance function parameterized by $\theta .$
For example, we might have

$$\sigma _ { i j } \left( \theta \right) = \theta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \exp \left\{ - | s _ { i } - s _ { j } | / \theta _ { 2 } \right\} ,$$

where $\theta _ { 2 }$ has the physical dimension of length and $\theta _ { 1 }$ has the same
physical dimension as $y .$ If $\theta$ is the parameter of interest and $\beta$
is regarded as a nuisance parameter, we may eliminate $\beta$ from the
likelihood by working with the set of contrasts,

$$\mathrm { R } = \left( \mathrm { I } - \mathrm { P } _ { X } \right) \mathrm { Y } = \left( \mathrm { I } - \mathrm { X } \left( \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { X } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \right) \mathrm { Y } ,$$

whose mean is zero and whose distribution does not depend on
$\beta .$ Any complete set of $n \quad - \quad p$ linearly independent contrasts with
zero mean is a linear transformation of $\mathrm { R } ,$ so that the choice of
projection matrix, $P _ { X } ,$ is immaterial. In other words, we could
replace $P _ { X }$ by $X \left( X ^ { T } \widetilde { W } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { T } W ,$ for any positive definite matrix
$\mathrm { W } ,$ without affecting the likelihood. See Exercises 7.10-7.13. In
this example there appears to be no loss of information on $\theta$ by
using $\mathrm { R }$ in place of $\mathrm { Y } ,$ though it is difficult to give a totally
satisfactory justification of this claim.

Although $\mathrm { R }$ has a rank-deficient covariance matrix whose de-
terminant is zero, it is still possible to write down explicitly the
$\log$ likelihood for $\theta$ based on $\mathrm { R } .$ The usual method (Kitanidis,
1987), which is to choose an arbitrary full-rank sub-vector, intro-
duces unnecessary and undesirable asymmetry into the formulae.
Assuming that $\Sigma$ has rank $n$ and that $X$ has rank $p ,$ the marginal
$\log$ likelihood for $\theta$ based on $\mathrm { R }$ is

$$l \left( \theta ; \mathrm { R } \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \det \Sigma - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \det \left( \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { X } \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q _ { 2 } \left( \mathrm { R } \right) ,$$

where

$$Q _ { 2 } \left( R \right) = R ^ { T } \left( \Sigma ^ { - 1 } - \Sigma ^ { - 1 } X \left( X ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } \right) R ,$$

the weighted residual sum of squares for $\mathrm { R } ,$ is unaffected by
the choice of projection matrix. An equivalent expression in
terms of the eigenvalues of the matrix $\left( I - P _ { X } \right) \Sigma \left( I - P _ { X } \right)$ is
given by Patterson and Thompson (1971). Yet another equivalent
expression is given by Harville (1974, 1977).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="248" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

Note that in the important special case where $\Sigma = \sigma ^ { 2 } I ,$ this
marginal log likelihood becomes

$$- \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( n - p \right) \log \sigma ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \mathrm { R S S } \right) / \sigma ^ { 2 }$$

where RSS is the residual sum of squares. This is just the marginal
$\log$ likelihood derived from $\mathrm { R S S } \sim \sigma ^ { 2 } \chi _ { n - p } ^ { 2 } .$

For a derivation of the marginal log likelihood, also sometimes
called the restricted log likelihood (Corbeil and Searle, 1976), see
Exercises 7.8-7.13.


#### 7.2.2 Conditional likelihood

Suppose that the parameter vector $\theta$ can be partitioned into
components $\theta = \left( \psi , \lambda \right) ,$ in which $\psi$ is the parameter vector of
interest and $\lambda$ is a vector of nuisance parameters. Suppose in
addition that for each fixed value $\psi _ { 0 }$ of $\psi ,$ the statistic $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ is
sufficient for $\lambda$ and complete. For definitions of completeness and
sufficiency, see Cox and Hinkley (1974), section 2.2 or Lehmann
(1986), sections 1.9 and 4.3. It is essential to distinguish two
cases, (i) where $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ depends on $\psi _ { 0 } ,$ and (ii) where $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ is
independent of $\psi _ { 0 }$ so that the sufficient statistic for $\lambda$ is the same
for all $\psi _ { 0 } .$ In (i) the conditional distribution of $\mathrm { Y }$ given $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ is
independent of $\lambda$ only under $\psi = \psi _ { 0 } .$ Thus we write

$$f _ { Y | S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) } \left( y | S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) ; \psi , \lambda \right)$$

for the conditional distribution.

In (ii), where $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) \equiv S _ { \lambda } ,$ we write the conditional density in
the form

$$f _ { Y \mid S _ { \lambda } } \left( y \mid S _ { \lambda } ; \psi \right)$$

emphasizing that the conditional distribution is independent of $\lambda .$
Here there is no conceptual difficulty in using

$$l _ { c } \left( \psi \right) = \log f _ { Y | S _ { \lambda } } \left( y | S _ { \lambda } ; \psi \right) = \log f _ { Y } \left( y ; \psi , \lambda \right) - \log f _ { S _ { \lambda } } \left( s _ { \lambda } ; \psi , \lambda \right)$$
(7.1)

as the conditional log likelihood for $\psi .$ The maximizing value
$\widehat { \psi } _ { \mathrm { c } } ,$ and the Fisher information $\mathrm { i } _ { \Psi } | _ { S _ { \lambda } } ,$ based on the conditional log
likelihood (7.1) are, in general, different from those derived from

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="249" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.2 MARGINAL AND CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

the full likelihood. For a simple but important example of this
conditioning argument, see sections 7.2.1 and 7.2.2.

When $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ depends on $\psi _ { 0 } ,$ however, it is most important
in writing the conditional density to distinguish between the two
values of $\psi .$ Thus

$$l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) = \log f _ { Y \mid S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) } \left( y \mid S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) ; \psi , \lambda \right) ,$$

considered as a function of $\psi$ and $\lambda$ for fixed $\psi _ { 0 } ,$ is a log-likelihood
function. For instance, under the usual regularity conditions, the
conditional score statistic

$$\frac { \partial l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) } { \partial \psi }$$

has zero expectation given $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ and conditional covariance
matrix equal to

1

$$- E \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) } { \partial \psi ^ { 2 } } \right) .$$

It follows that the score statistic is uncorrelated with $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ for
all parameter values.

By contrast, the reduced function

$$l ^ { * } \left( \psi \right) = l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi \right)$$

is not the logarithm of a density with respect to any fixed measure,
and hence does not ordinarily have the properties of a log-likelihood
function. The reason for this is that the transformation from
the original variables $\mathrm { Y }$ to the pair $\left( S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) , \bar { S } \right) ,$ where $\overrightarrow { S }$ is a
complementary statistic, involves a Jacobian that depends on $\psi _ { 0 } .$
So long as $\psi _ { 0 }$ is regarded as fixed, the Jacobian has no effect
on the $\log$ likelihood and can be ignored. But if the Jacobian
depends on $\psi$ an extra term must be included in the log likelihood.
Ordinarily, therefore, when computing likelihood functions it does
not make sense to condition on parameter-dependent statistics. See
Exercises 7.1-7.6 for several examples in which these differences are
important.

Ideally we would like to choose a value $\psi _ { 0 }$ for the conditioning
statistic that is as near as possible to the conditional maximum-
likelihood estimate, $\widehat { \psi } _ { \mathrm { c } } .$ For purposes of estimation, this effect is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="250" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

achieved by solving the conditional likelihood equation $U _ { \psi } = 0 ,$
where

$$U _ { \psi } = \frac { \partial l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) } { \partial \psi } | _ { \psi _ { 0 } = \psi }$$
(7.2)

evaluated at $\widehat { \lambda } \left( \psi \right) .$ This is not the same as finding the roots of
$\partial l ^ { * } \left( \psi \right) / \partial \psi = 0$ because the log likelihood is differentiated only with
respect to the first argument. Another way of deriving (7.2) is to
bias-correct the unconditional log-likelihood derivatives as follows:

$$\frac { \partial l \left( \psi , \lambda \right) } { \partial \psi } - E \left( \frac { \partial l \left( \psi , \lambda \right) } { \partial \psi } | S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi \right) \right) .$$

This bias-corrected derivative is identical to (7.2) with $\lambda$ replaced
by $\widehat { \lambda } \left( \psi \right) .$ This interpretation via estimating functions has been
emphasized by Godambe (1976) and by Lindsay (1982).

The asymptotic variance of $\widehat { \psi } _ { c }$ is the inverse of

$$- E \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) } { \partial \psi ^ { 2 } } \right) | _ { \psi _ { o } = \psi }$$

evaluated at $\widehat { \lambda } \left( \psi \right) .$

The conditional score function as defined above is unaffected by
the parameterization chosen for the nuisance parameters because

$$\frac { \partial l _ { c } \left( \psi , \lambda ; \psi \right) } { \partial \lambda } \equiv 0$$

for all parameter values. Consequently there is no ambiguity
regarding the meaning of (7.2).

This line of argument produces a usable score statistic having
zero mean at the true parameter point, a 'conditional likelihood'
estimator and an approximate standard error, but it does not
produce a likelihood function for $\psi$ directly. For this purpose the
modified profile likelihood of Barndorff-Nielsen (1985, 1986) may
be used. For a related derivation via orthogonal parameters, see
Cox and Reid (1987).

The following example illustrates several aspects of the condi-
tioning argument. Suppose that

and
$$Y _ { 1 } \sim N \left( \mu _ { 1 } , 1 \right)$$
$$Y _ { 2 } \sim N \left( \mu _ { 2 } , 1 \right)$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="251" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.2 MARGINAL AND CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

are independent and that the ratio $\psi = \mu _ { 2 } / \mu _ { 1 }$ is the parameter of
interest. To complete the parameterization, we may take $\lambda _ { 1 } = \mu _ { 1 } ,$
$\lambda _ { 2 } = \mu _ { 2 }$ or any other suitable complementary parameter such as
$\lambda _ { 3 } = \mu _ { 1 } + \mu _ { 2 }$ or the orthogonal parameter $\left( \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } .$ A sufficient
statistic for the nuisance parameter given $\psi = \psi _ { 0 }$ is

$$S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) = Y _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 0 } Y _ { 2 } .$$

Other equivalent forms of the sufficient statistic are

$$\widehat { \mu } _ { 1 } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) = \widehat { \lambda } _ { 1 } = \left( Y _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 0 } Y _ { 2 } \right) / \left( 1 + \psi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$\widehat { \mu } _ { 2 } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right) = \widehat { \lambda } _ { 2 } = \psi _ { 0 } \left( Y _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 0 } Y _ { 2 } \right) / \left( 1 + \psi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$

and $\widehat { \lambda } _ { 3 } = \left( 1 + \psi _ { 0 } \right) \left( Y _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 0 } Y _ { 2 } \right) / \left( 1 + \psi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) .$

The conditional log likelihood given $S _ { \lambda } \left( \psi _ { 0 } \right)$ is

$$l _ { c } \left( \psi , \mu _ { 1 } ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \left( y _ { 2 } - \psi _ { 0 } y _ { 1 } - \left( \psi - \psi _ { 0 } \right) \mu _ { 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \psi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \left( 1 + \psi _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \right) .$$

Differentiation with respect to $\psi$ followed by setting $\psi _ { 0 } = \psi$ gives

$$U _ { \psi } = \frac { \partial l _ { c } \left( \psi , \mu _ { 1 } ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) } { \partial \psi } | _ { \psi _ { 0 } = \psi } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } \left( y _ { 2 } - \psi _ { W 1 } \right) } { 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } } .$$
(7.3)

Note that the Jacobian term vanishes on differentiation. Further,

$$E \left( U _ { \psi } \right) = 0 ,$$
$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( U _ { \psi } \right) = \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } / \left( 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } \right) ,$$
$$- E \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l _ { c } \left( \psi , \mu _ { 1 } ; \psi _ { 0 } \right) } { \partial \psi ^ { 2 } } \right) | _ { \psi _ { 0 } = \psi } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } } ,$$

so that the usual likelihood properties are satisfied. Also $\widehat { \psi } _ { c } =$
$y _ { 2 } / y _ { 1 }$ with 'asymptotic' variance $\left( 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } \right) / \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ,$ the usual approxima-
tion for the variance of a ratio estimator. In all of these expressions
$\mu _ { 1 }$ is to be replaced by $\widehat { \mu } _ { 1 } \left( \psi \right) .$

Normal approximation for the distribution of the ratio is unsat-
isfactory unless $\mu _ { 1 }$ is large compared to the standard deviation of
$Y _ { 1 } .$ Fieller confidence intervals generated via the score statistic $U _ { \psi }$
by

$$\left\{ \psi : U _ { \psi } ^ { 2 } / \mathrm { v a r } \left( U _ { \psi } \right) \leq k _ { \alpha / 2 } ^ { 2 } \right\}$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="252" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

are exact and are preferred over Normal approximations.

By contrast, differentiation of the reduced function $l ^ { * } \left( \cdot \right)$ gives

$$\frac { \partial l _ { c } \left( \psi , \mu _ { 1 } ; \psi \right) } { \partial \psi } = \frac { y _ { 1 } \left( y _ { 2 } - \psi y _ { 1 } \right) } { 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \psi \left( y _ { 2 } - \psi y _ { 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { \psi } { 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } }$$

The latter derivative has mean $- \psi / \left( 1 + \psi ^ { 2 } \right) .$ For further discussion
of this point see Exercise 7.19.


#### 7.2.3 Exponential-family models

Suppose that the log likelihood for $\theta = \left( \psi , \lambda \right)$ can be written in
the exponential-family form

$$l \left( \theta ; y \right) = \theta ^ { T } s - b \left( \theta \right)$$

and admits a decomposition of the form

$$l \left( \theta ; y \right) = \psi ^ { T } s _ { 1 } + \lambda ^ { T } s _ { 2 } - b \left( \psi , \lambda \right) ,$$
(7.4)

where $\mathrm { s } = \left( \mathrm { s } _ { 1 } \left( y \right) , \mathrm { s } _ { 2 } \left( y \right) \right)$ are functions of the data. Likelihood
functions of this type occur most commonly when the observations
are independent and the model considered is linear in the canonical
parameter. Then the sufficient statistic is a linear function of the
data, namely $s = X ^ { T } y .$ A decomposition of the form (7.4) can be
achieved only if the parameter of interest, $\psi ,$ is a linear function
of $\theta .$ The choice of nuisance parameter, $\lambda ,$ is to a large extent
arbitrary and the inferences regarding $\psi$ should be unaffected by
the parameterization chosen for $\lambda .$

It may be helpful at this stage to consider a simple example.
Suppose that $Y _ { 1 } ,$ $Y _ { 2 }$ are independent Poisson random variables with
means $\mu _ { 1 } ,$ $\mu _ { 2 }$ and that we are interested in the ratio $\psi ^ { \prime } = \mu _ { 1 } / \mu _ { 2 } .$
Here $\theta _ { i } = \log \mu _ { i }$ are the canonical parameters, and the parameter
of interest $\psi = \log \psi ^ { \prime } = \theta _ { 1 } - \theta _ { 2 }$ is a linear contrast of the canonical
parameters. For the nuisance parameter, we may choose any one
of $a$ variety of complementary parameters such as

$$\lambda _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = \mu _ { 1 } , \quad \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \mu _ { 2 } , \quad \lambda _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = \mu _ { 1 } + \mu _ { 2 } \quad \mathrm { o r }$$
14=μιμ2.

The $\log$ likelihood expressed initially in terms of $\theta$ is

$$l \left( \theta ; y \right) = y _ { 1 } \theta _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \theta _ { 2 } - \exp \left( \theta _ { 1 } \right) - \exp \left( \theta _ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \lambda _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } \psi - \exp \left( \lambda _ { 1 } \right) \left( 1 + e ^ { - \psi } \right)$$
$$= \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \lambda _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } \psi - \exp \left( \lambda _ { 2 } \right) \left( 1 + e ^ { \psi } \right)$$
$$= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \lambda _ { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } \right) \psi - 2 \exp \left( \lambda _ { 4 } / 2 \right) \cosh \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \psi \right) ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="253" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.2 MARGINAL AND CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

where $\psi = \log \psi ^ { \prime }$ and $\lambda _ { j } = \log \lambda _ { j } ^ { \prime } .$

It follows from (7.4) that for any given value of $\psi ,$ $2$ is
sufficient for the nuisance parameter and that $s _ { 2 }$ is the same
whatever parameterization is chosen for the nuisance parameter.
In the above example, $\mathrm { s } _ { 2 } = Y _ { \bullet } .$ for each of the parameterizations
considered. The conditional distribution of the data $\mathrm { Y }$ given $\mathrm { s } _ { 2 }$
does not depend on $\lambda$ and hence the conditional log likelihood

$$l \left( \psi | s _ { 2 } \right) = \psi ^ { T } s _ { 1 } - b ^ { * } \left( \psi ; s _ { 2 } \right)$$
(7.5)

may be used for inferences regarding $\psi .$ Given the sufficient
statistic $\mathrm { s } _ { 2 } ,$ the value of the nuisance parameter is irrelevant in
subsequent calculations.

Note that the conditional $\log$ likelihood retains the exponential-
family form in which $\mathrm { s } _ { 1 }$ is conditionally sufficient for $\psi$ given the
value of $\mathrm { s } _ { 2 } .$ In the Poisson example, the required conditional
distribution is that of the pair $\left( Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } \right)$ given that $Y _ { * } = m ,$ and this
is well known to yield the binomial distribution. It is immaterial
here whether we work with the conditional distribution of $Y _ { 1 } | Y _ { . } ,$
$Y _ { 2 } | Y _ { 0 } .$ or $Y _ { 1 } - Y _ { 2 } | Y _ { 0 } .$ because these conditional distributions differ
by a fixed linear transformation.

For a second example, suppose that $Y _ { 1 } \sim B \left( 1 , \pi _ { 1 } \right)$ and $Y _ { 2 }$ ~
$B \left( 1 , \pi _ { 2 } \right)$ are independent and that the odds ratio

$$\psi ^ { \prime } = \frac { \pi _ { 1 } } { 1 - \pi _ { 1 } } / \frac { \pi _ { 2 } } { 1 - \pi _ { 2 } }$$

is the parameter of interest. The log-likelihood function is

$$y _ { 1 } \log \left( \frac { \pi _ { 1 } } { 1 - \pi _ { 1 } } \right) + y _ { 2 } \log \left( \frac { \pi _ { 2 } } { 1 - \pi _ { 2 } } \right) + \log \left( 1 - \pi _ { 1 } \right) + \log \left( 1 - \pi _ { 2 } \right)$$
$$= y _ { 1 } \psi + \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) \log \left( \frac { \pi _ { 2 } } { 1 - \pi _ { 2 } } \right) + \log \left( 1 - \pi _ { 1 } \right) + \log \left( 1 - \pi _ { 2 } \right)$$

where $\psi = \log \psi ^ { \prime } .$ By the argument just given, we are led to
consider the conditional distribution of $\left( Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } \right)$ given that $Y _ { 0 } = y .$
If $y _ { 0 } = 0$ or $y _ { 0 } = 2$ the conditional distribution is degenerate.
Otherwise if $y _ { * } = 1 ,$ we have

$$\mathrm { p r } \left( Y _ { 1 } = 0 | Y _ { \cdot } = 1 \right) = 1 / \left( 1 + \psi ^ { \prime } \right)$$
$$\mathrm { p r } \left( Y _ { 1 } = 1 | Y _ { \cdot } = 1 \right) = \psi ^ { \prime } / \left( 1 + \psi ^ { \prime } \right)$$

This is a particular instance of the hypergeometric distribution
studied in section 7.3.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="254" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->


#### 7.2.4 Profile likelihood

In those instances where they exist, marginal and conditional
likelihoods work well, often with little sacrifice of information.
However, marginal and conditional likelihoods are available only
in very special problems. The profile $\log$ likelihood, while less
satisfactory from several points of view, does have the important
virtue that it can be used in all circumstances.

Let $\widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi }$ be the maximum-likelihood estimate of $\lambda$ for fixed $\psi .$
This maximum is assumed here to be unique, as it is for most
generalized linear models. The partially maximized log-likelihood
function,

$$l ^ { \dagger } \left( \psi ; y \right) = l \left( \psi , \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } ; y \right) = \sup _ { \lambda } l \left( \psi , \lambda ; y \right)$$

is called the profile $\log$ likelihood for $\psi .$ Under certain conditions
the profile log likelihood may be used just like any other log
likelihood. In particular, the maximum of $l ^ { \dagger } \left( \psi ; y \right)$ coincides with
the overall maximum-likelihood estimate. Further, approximate
confidence sets for $\psi$ may be obtained in the usual way, namely

$$\left\{ \psi : 2 l ^ { \dagger } \left( \widehat { \psi } ; y \right) - 2 l ^ { \dagger } \left( \psi ; y \right) \leq \chi _ { p , 1 - \alpha } ^ { 2 } \right\}$$

where $p = \dim \left( \psi \right) .$ Alternatively, though usually less accurately,
intervals may be based on $\widehat { \psi }$ together with the second derivatives
of $l ^ { \dagger } \left( \psi ; y \right)$ at the maximum. Such confidence intervals are often
satisfactory if $\dim \left( \lambda \right)$ is small in relation to the total Fisher
information, but are liable to be misleading otherwise.

Unfortunately $l ^ { \dagger } \left( \psi ; y \right)$ is not a log likelihood function in the
usual sense. Most obviously, its derivative does not have zero mean,
a property that is essential for estimating equations. In fact the
derivative of $l ^ { \dagger }$ may be written in terms of the partial derivatives
of $l$ as follows:

$$\frac { \partial l ^ { \dagger } } { \partial \psi } = \frac { \partial } { \partial \psi } l \left( \psi , \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } ; y \right)$$
$$= \frac { \partial l } { \partial \psi } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l } { \partial \psi \partial \lambda } \left( \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } - \lambda \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial ^ { 3 } l } { \partial \psi \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } } \left( \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } - \lambda \right) ^ { 2 } + \ldots .$$
$$+ \left\{ \frac { \partial l } { \partial \lambda } + \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l } { \partial \lambda ^ { 2 } } \left( \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } - \lambda \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial ^ { 3 } l } { \partial \lambda ^ { 3 } } \left( \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } - \lambda \right) ^ { 2 } + \ldots \right\} \frac { \partial \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } } { \partial \psi } .$$

The expression in parentheses is just $\partial l \left( \psi , \lambda \right) / \partial \lambda$ evaluated at $\widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } ,$
and hence is identically zero. Under the usual regularity conditions

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="255" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.3 HYPERGEOMETRIC DISTRIBUTION" -->

for large n, the remaining three terms are $O _ { p } \left( n ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) ,$ $O _ { p } \left( n ^ { 1 / 2 } \right)$ and
$O _ { p } \left( 1 \right)$ respectively. The first term has zero mean but the remaining
two have mean $O \left( 1 \right)$ if $\widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi }$ is a consistent estimate of $\lambda .$ Their
expectations may be inflated if $\widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi }$ is not consistent.

A simple expression for the approximate mean of $\partial l ^ { \dagger } / \partial \psi$ in terms
of cumulants of the derivatives of $l$ is given by McCullagh and
Tibshirani (1988).

In general, if the dimension of $\lambda$ is a substantial fraction of $n$ n,
the mean of $\partial l ^ { \dagger } / \partial \psi$ is not negligible and the profile log likelihood
can be misleading if interpreted as an ordinary log likelihood.

It is interesting to compare the profile log likelihood with the
marginal log likelihood in a model for which both can be calculated
explicitly. The covariance-estimation model, considered briefly at
the end of section 7.2.1, is such an example. The profile $\log$
likelihood for the covariance parameters $\theta$ in that problem is

$$l ^ { \dagger } \left( \theta ; y \right) = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \det \Sigma - \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q _ { 2 } \left( \mathrm { R } \right) ,$$

which differs from the marginal log likelihood given at the end of
section 7.2.1 by the term $\frac { 1 } { 2 } \log \det \left( X ^ { T } \Sigma ^ { - 1 } X \right) .$ Both the marginal
and profile log likelihoods depend on the data only through the
contrasts or residuals, $\mathrm { R } .$ The marginal log likelihood is clearly
preferable to $l ^ { \dagger }$ in this example, because $l ^ { \dagger }$ is not a $\log$ likelihood.
The derivatives of $l ^ { \dagger } ,$ unlike those of the marginal log likelihood,
do not have zero mean.

The use of profile likelihoods for the estimation of covariance
functions has been studied by Mardia and Marshall (1984).


### 7.3 Hypergeometric distributions


#### 7.3.1 Central hypergeometric distribution

Suppose that a simple random sample of size $m _ { 1 }$ is taken from a
population of size $m$ .. The population is known to comprise $s _ { 1 }$
individuals who have attribute $A$ and $s _ { 2 } = m _ { * } - s _ { 1 }$ who do not.
In the sample, $Y$ individuals have attribute $A$ and the remainder,
$m _ { 1 } - Y ,$ do not. The following table gives the numbers of sampled
and non-sampled subjects who possess the attribute in question.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="256" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->


<table>
<caption>Attribute</caption>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th>$A$</th>
<th>$\bar { A }$</th>
<th>Total</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>sampled</td>
<td>$Y \equiv Y _ { 1 1 }$</td>
<td>$m _ { 1 } - Y \equiv Y _ { 1 2 }$</td>
<td>$m _ { 1 }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>non-sampled</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td>$m _ { 2 }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Total</td>
<td>$s _ { 1 }$</td>
<td>$s _ { 2 }$</td>
<td>$m _ { * } \equiv s .$</td>
</tr>
</table>


Under the simple random sampling model, the distribution of $Y$
conditionally on the marginal totals $m ,$ $s$ is

$$\mathrm { p r } \left( Y = y | \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { s } \right) = \frac { \binom { m _ { 1 } } { y } \binom { m _ { 2 } } { s _ { 1 } - y } } { \binom { m _ { 1 } } { s _ { 1 } } } = \frac { \binom { s _ { 1 } } { y } \binom { s _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } - y } } { \binom { s _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } }$$
(7.6)

The range of possible values for $y$ is the set of integers satisfying

$$a = \max \left( 0 , s _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } \right) \leq y \leq \min \left( m _ { 1 } , s _ { 1 } \right) = b .$$
(7.7)

There are $\min \left( m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } \right) + 1$ points in the sample space. If
$a = b ,$ the conditional distribution puts all its mass at the single
point a. Degeneracy occurs only if one of the four marginal totals
is zero.

The central hypergeometric distribution (7.6) is denoted by
$Y \sim H \left( \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { s } \right)$ or by $Y \sim H \left( \mathrm { s } , \mathrm { m } \right) .$

An alternative derivation of the hypergeometric distribution is
as follows. Suppose that $Y _ { 1 } \sim B \left( m _ { 1 } , \pi \right)$ and $Y _ { 2 } \sim B \left( m _ { 2 } , \pi \right)$
are independent binomial random variables. Then the conditional
distribution of $Y \equiv Y _ { 1 }$ conditionally on $Y _ { 1 } + Y _ { 2 } = s _ { 1 }$ is given by
(7.6).

The descending factorial moments of $Y$ are easily obtained from
(7.6) as follows:

$$\mu _ { \left[ r \right] } = E \left\{ Y ^ { \left( r \right) } \right\} = m _ { 1 } ^ { \left( r \right) } s _ { 1 } ^ { \left( r \right) } / m _ { . } ^ { \left( r \right) } ,$$

where $Y ^ { \left( r \right) } = Y \left( Y - 1 \right) \ldots \left( Y - r + 1 \right) ,$ provided that $r \leq$
$\min \left( m _ { 1 } , s _ { 1 } \right) .$ From these factorial moments we may compute the
cumulants of $Y$ as follows. First, define the following functions
of the marginal frequencies in terms of the sampling fraction
$\tau = m _ { 1 } / m .$

$$K _ { 1 } = s _ { 1 } / m _ { 0 } ,$$
$$\lambda _ { 1 } = m _ { . } \tau _ { 1 } = m _ { 1 } ,$$
$$K _ { 2 } = s _ { 1 } s _ { s } / m _ { 0 } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } ,$$
$$\lambda _ { 2 } = m _ { . } \tau _ { 1 } \left( 1 - \tau _ { 1 } \right) = m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } / m _ { . } ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="257" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.3 HYPERGEOMETRIC DISTRIBUTION" -->

$$K _ { 3 } = s _ { 1 } s _ { 2 } \left( s _ { 2 } - s _ { 1 } \right) / m _ { . } ^ { \left( 3 \right) } , \quad \lambda _ { 3 } = m _ { . } \tau _ { 1 } \left( 1 - \tau _ { 1 } \right) \left( 1 - 2 \tau _ { 1 } \right)$$
$$= m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } \left( m _ { 2 } - m _ { 1 } \right) / m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ,$$
$$K _ { 4 } = s _ { 1 } s _ { 2 } \left\{ m _ { \bullet } \left( m _ { \bullet } + 1 \right) - 6 s _ { 1 } s _ { 2 } \right\} / m _ { \bullet } ^ { \left( 4 \right) } ,$$
$$\lambda _ { 4 } = m _ { \cdot } \tau _ { 1 } \left( 1 - \tau _ { 1 } \right) \left( 1 - 6 \tau _ { 1 } \left( 1 - \tau _ { 1 } \right) \right) .$$
$K _ { 2 2 } = s _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } s _ { 2 } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } / m _ { . } ^ { \left( 4 \right) } ,$

The first four cumulants of $Y$ are

$$E \left( Y \right) = K _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 1 } , \quad \mathrm { v a r } \left( Y \right) = K _ { 2 } \lambda _ { 2 } ,$$ ☒ ☒

$$\kappa _ { 3 } \left( Y \right) = K _ { 3 } \lambda _ { 3 } , \quad \kappa _ { 4 } \left( Y \right) = K _ { 4 } \lambda _ { 4 } - 6 K _ { 2 2 } \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } / \left( m _ { * } - 1 \right) .$$
(7.8)
☒ ☒

Note that $\lambda _ { r } ,$ is the rth cumulant of the $B \left( m , \tau _ { 1 } \right)$ distribution
associated with the sampling fraction, whereas $K _ { 1 } , \ldots , K _ { 4 } ,$ $K _ { 2 2 }$
are the population k-statistics and polykay up to order four.
Details of these symmetric functions are given in McCullagh (1987),
Chapter 4, especially section 4.6. For large $m .$ and for fixed
sampling fraction, the $\lambda \mathrm { s }$ are $O \left( m _ { * } \right) ,$ whereas the $K \mathrm { s }$ are $O \left( 1 \right)$
for fixed attribute ratio, $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 } .$

Note that the third cumulant of $Y$ is zero if either $K _ { 3 } = 0$
or $\lambda _ { 3 } = 0 .$ In fact all odd-order cumulants are zero under these
conditions and the distribution of $Y$ is symmetric.


#### 7.3.2 Non-central hypergeometric distribution

The non-central hypergeometric distribution with odds ratio $\psi$ is
an exponentially weighted version of the central hypergeometric
distribution (7.6). Thus

$$\mathrm { p r } \left( Y = y ; \psi \right) = \frac { \binom { m _ { 1 } } { y } \binom { m _ { 2 } } { s _ { 1 } - y } \psi ^ { y } } { P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) }$$
(7.9)

where $P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right)$ is the polynomial in $\psi ,$

$$P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) = \sum _ { j = a } ^ { b } \left( \begin{array}{} m _ { 1 } \\ j \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} m _ { 2 } \\ s _ { 1 } - j \end{array} \right) \psi ^ { j } .$$

The range of summation is given by (7.7). This distribution arises
in the exponentially weighted sampling scheme in which each of
the $\binom { m _ { \bullet } } { m _ { 1 } }$ possible samples is weighted proportionally to $\psi ^ { y } ,$ where

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="258" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

$y$ is a particular function of the sample. Here $y$ is the number of
individuals in the sample who possess attribute $A ,$ but in principle
any function of the sample could be chosen.

Alternatively, the non-central hypergeometric distribution may
be derived as follows. Suppose that $Y _ { 1 } \sim B \left( m _ { 1 } , \pi _ { 1 } \right) ,$ $Y _ { 2 }$ ~
$B \left( m _ { 2 } , \pi _ { 2 } \right)$ are independent binomial random variables and that
$\psi = \pi _ { 1 } \left( 1 - \pi _ { 2 } \right) / \left\{ \pi _ { 2 } \left( 1 - \pi _ { 1 } \right) \right\}$ is the odds ratio. Then the conditional
distribution of $Y _ { 1 }$ given that $Y _ { 1 } = s _ { 1 }$ is non-central hypergeometric
with parameter $\psi .$ For conciseness, we write $Y \sim H \left( \mathrm { m } , \mathrm { s } ; \psi \right)$ to
denote the conditional distribution (7.9). Note that $P _ { 0 } \left( 1 \right) = \binom { m _ { \bullet } } { s _ { 1 } } ,$
so that $H \left( m , s ; 1 \right)$ is identical to $H \left( m , s \right) .$

An 'observation' from the distribution (7.9) is often presented
as a $2 \times 2$ table in which the marginal totals are $m$ and s. The
contribution of such an observation to the conditional log likelihood
is

$$y \log \psi - \log P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) ,$$

where the dependence on $m$ and $s$ has been suppressed in the
notation for the polynomial $P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) .$ This log likelihood has the
standard exponential-family form with canonical parameter $\theta =$
$\log \psi$ and cumulant function

$$K \left( \theta \right) = \log P _ { 0 } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) .$$

The mean and variance of $Y$ are therefore

$$\kappa _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) = E \left( Y ; \theta \right) = K ^ { \prime } \left( \theta \right) = P _ { 1 } \left( \psi \right) / P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right)$$
$$\kappa _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right) = \mathrm { v a r } \left( Y ; \theta \right) = K ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \theta \right) = P _ { 2 } \left( \psi \right) / P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) - \left\{ P _ { 1 } \left( \psi \right) / P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) \right\} ^ { 2 } ,$$

where $P _ { r } \left( \psi \right)$ is the polynomial

$$P _ { r } \left( \psi \right) = \sum _ { j = a } ^ { b } j ^ { r } \psi ^ { j } \left( \begin{array}{} m _ { 1 } \\ j \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} m _ { 2 } \\ s _ { 1 } - j \end{array} \right) .$$
(7.10)

More generally, the moments about the origin are expressible as
rational functions in $\psi ,$ namely

$$\mu _ { r } \left( \psi \right) = P _ { r } \left( \psi \right) / P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="259" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.3 HYPERGEOMETRIC DISTRIBUTION" -->

Unfortunately the functions $\kappa _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right)$ and $\kappa _ { 2 } \left( \theta \right)$ are awkward to
compute particularly if the range of summation in (7.10) is exten-
sive. The following approximations are often useful. First, it is
easily shown that, conditionally on the marginal totals,

$$E \left( Y _ { 1 1 } Y _ { 2 2 } \right) = \psi E \left( Y _ { 1 2 } Y _ { 2 1 } \right)$$

and, more generally, that

$$E \left( Y _ { 1 1 } ^ { \left( r \right) } Y _ { 2 2 } ^ { \left( r \right) } \right) = \psi ^ { r } E \left( Y _ { 1 2 } ^ { \left( r \right) } Y _ { 2 1 } ^ { \left( r \right) } \right) .$$

Hence, since $E \left( Y _ { 1 1 } Y _ { 2 2 } \right) = \mu _ { 1 1 } \mu _ { 2 2 } + \kappa _ { 2 } ,$ we have

$$\psi = \frac { \mu _ { 1 1 } \mu _ { 2 2 } + \kappa _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 2 } \mu _ { 2 1 } + \kappa _ { 2 } } ,$$

where $\mu _ { 1 1 } = E \left( Y _ { 1 1 } ; \theta \right) , \ldots .$ are the conditional means for the four
cells, and $\kappa _ { 2 }$ is the conditional variance of each cell. Consequently
we have the following exact relationship between $\kappa _ { 1 } \equiv \mu _ { 1 1 }$ and $\kappa _ { 2 } :$

$$\kappa _ { 1 } \left( m _ { 2 } - s _ { 1 } + \kappa _ { 1 } \right) + \kappa _ { 2 } = \psi \left\{ \left( s _ { 1 } - \kappa _ { 1 } \right) \left( m _ { 1 } - \kappa _ { 1 } \right) + \kappa _ { 2 } \right\} .$$
(7.11)

In addition, the following approximate relationship may be derived
from asymptotic considerations of the type discussed in section
6.5.6:

$$\kappa _ { 2 } \simeq \frac { m _ { \cdot } } { m _ { \cdot } - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { 1 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { 1 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { 2 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { 2 2 } } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$$
(7.12)

In addition to being asymptotically correct for large $b \quad - \quad a ,$ this
expression is exact for $m _ { * } = 2 ,$ the smallest non-degenerate value,
and also for $\psi = 1 ,$ whatever the marginal configuration.

The simultaneous solution to (7.11) and (7.12) gives a very
accurate approximation to the conditional mean and variance
provided that either $| \theta | < 2$ or the marginal totals are large: see
Breslow and Cologne (1986). An equally accurate but slightly more
complicated approximation is given by Barndorff-Nielsen and Cox
(1979).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="260" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->


#### 7.3.3 Multivariate hypergeometric distribution

Suppose that $\mathrm { Y } _ { 1 } \sim M \left( m _ { 1 } , \pi \right)$ and $Y _ { 2 } \sim M \left( m _ { 2 } , \pi \right)$ are independent
multinomial vectors, each on $k$ categories. Then the conditional
distribution of the vector $Y = Y _ { 1 }$ given that $Y _ { 1 } + Y _ { 2 } = s$ is as
follows.

$$\mathrm { p r } \left( \mathrm { Y } = \mathrm { y } | \mathrm { s } \right) = \frac { \binom { m _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { y } } \binom { m _ { 2 } } { \mathrm { s } - \mathrm { y } } } { \binom { m _ { \mathrm { s } } } { \mathrm { s } } } = \frac { \binom { s _ { 1 } } { y } } { \binom { m } { \mathrm { s } } } = \frac { \binom { s _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \cdots \binom { s _ { k } } { y _ { k } } } { \binom { s _ { \mathrm { s } } } { y _ { 0 } } }$$
(7.13)

where $s _ { * } \equiv m _ { * } .$ and $y _ { 0 } \equiv m _ { 1 } .$ From a statistical perspective, one
important aspect of this conditional distribution is that it does not
depend on the multinomial probability vector $\pi .$

$A n$ alternative derivation of (7.13) based on simple random
sampling from a finite population of size $m .$ is as follows. Suppose
that attribute $G$ has $k$ levels and that the $k$ levels of $G$ are mutually
exclusive and exhaustive. For instance $G$ might denote a particular
genetic marker such as blood group with levels $\mathrm { O } ,$ $A ,$ $\mathrm { B } ,$ $\mathrm { A B } .$ In a
different context, $G$ might denote the species of salmon in Lake
Michigan, with levels whose labels are coho, chinook, .... Under
simple random sampling, the distribution of species in a sample of
size $m _ { 1 }$ is given by (7.13), where $s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } , \ldots .$ are the numbers of coho,
chinook, ... in the lake.

If the sampled and non-sampled individuals are arranged in a
two-way table, the entries appear as follows.


<table>
<caption>Attribute level</caption>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th>$G _ { 1 }$</th>
<th>$G _ { 2 }$</th>
<th>... $G _ { k }$</th>
<th>Total</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Sampled</td>
<td>$Y _ { 1 1 }$</td>
<td>$Y _ { 1 2 }$</td>
<td>... $Y _ { 1 k }$</td>
<td>$m _ { 1 }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Not sampled</td>
<td>$Y _ { 2 1 }$</td>
<td>$Y _ { 2 2 }$</td>
<td>... $Y _ { 2 k }$</td>
<td>$m _ { 2 }$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Total</td>
<td>$s _ { 1 }$</td>
<td>$s _ { 2 }$</td>
<td>... $s _ { k }$</td>
<td>$m .$ $\equiv s .$</td>
</tr>
</table>


Evidently, in this table we may reverse the roles played by the
rows and the columns. Thus, suppose that $Y _ { 1 } \sim B \left( s _ { 1 } , \tau \right) , \ldots , Y _ { k } \sim$
$B \left( s _ { k } , \tau \right)$ are independent binomial random variables. Then the
joint conditional distribution of $\mathrm { Y } = \left( Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { k } \right) ,$ conditional on
the event $Y _ { * } = m _ { 1 } ,$ is again given by (7.13). Note that in the first
derivation, conditioning reduces the dimension of the sample space
from $2 \left( k - 1 \right)$ to $k - 1 ,$ in the process eliminating $k - 1$ parameters $\pi .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="261" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.3 HYPERGEOMETRIC DISTRIBUTION" -->

In the latter derivation, conditioning reduces the dimension from
$k$ to $k \quad - \quad 1 ,$ in the process eliminating the single parameter $\tau .$

The joint conditional mean vector and covariance matrix of $\mathrm { Y }$
are given by

$$E \left( Y _ { j } \right) = \widetilde { \pi } _ { j } m _ { 1 }$$
$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( Y _ { j } \right) = \widetilde { \pi } _ { j } \left( 1 - \widetilde { \pi } _ { j } \right) m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } / \left( m _ { \bullet } - 1 \right)$$
$$\mathrm { c o v } \left( Y _ { i } , Y _ { j } \right) = - \widetilde { \pi } _ { i } \widetilde { \pi } _ { j } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } / \left( m _ { . } - 1 \right) ,$$

where $\widetilde { \pi } _ { j } = s _ { j } / s .$ is the proportion in the population who have
attribute $j .$


#### 7.3.4 Multivariate non-central hypergeometric distribution

Suppose that $\mathrm { Y } _ { 1 } \sim M \left( m _ { 1 } , \pi _ { 1 } \right)$ and $\mathrm { Y } _ { 2 } \sim M \left( m _ { 2 } , \pi _ { 2 } \right)$ are indepen-
dent multinomial random variables on $k$ categories each. Then the
conditional distribution of $\mathrm { Y } \equiv \mathrm { Y } _ { 1 }$ given that $Y _ { 1 } + Y _ { 2 } = s$ is as
follows:

$$\mathrm { p r } \left( \mathrm { Y } = \mathrm { y } | \mathrm { s } , \psi \right) = \frac { \binom { m _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { y } } \binom { m _ { 2 } } { \mathrm { s } - \mathrm { y } } \psi _ { 1 } ^ { y _ { 1 } } \ldots \psi _ { k } ^ { y _ { k } } } { \sum _ { i } \binom { m _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { j } } \binom { m _ { 2 } } { \mathrm { s } - \mathrm { i } } \Psi _ { 1 } ^ { j _ { 1 } } \ldots \psi _ { k } ^ { j _ { k } } }$$
(7.14)

The sum in the denominator runs over the entire conditional sample
space, which comprises all non-negative integer-valued vectors $\mathrm { y }$
satisfying the positivity conditions

$$0 \leq y _ { j } \leq s _ { j } , \quad \sum y _ { j } = m _ { 1 } .$$

The odds-ratio parameters $\psi _ { j }$ are defined as contrasts relative to
category $k$ by

$$\psi _ { j } = \frac { \pi _ { 1 j } \pi _ { 2 k } } { \pi _ { 2 j } \pi _ { 1 k } }$$

so that $\psi _ { k } \equiv 1 .$

The exact non-central moments and cumulants are complicated
functions of $\psi$ and the marginal frequencies s. Direct computation
is awkward because of the nature of the sample space and because
of the large number of points it contains. The following equation,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="262" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

however, gives a simple exact relationship between the conditional
mean vector $\mu _ { 1 }$ of $Y$ and the conditional covariance matrix $\Sigma .$

$$\frac { E \left( Y _ { 1 j } Y _ { 2 k } \right) } { E \left( Y _ { 2 j } Y _ { 1 k } \right) } = \psi _ { j } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 j } \mu _ { 2 k } - \sigma _ { j k } } { \mu _ { 2 j } \mu _ { 1 k } - \sigma _ { j k } } .$$
(7.15)

Note that

$$\sigma _ { j k } = \mathrm { c o v } \left( Y _ { 1 j } , Y _ { 1 k } \right) = - \mathrm { c o v } \left( Y _ { 1 j } , Y _ { 2 k } \right)$$

is negative for $j < k .$

The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ of $Y _ { 1 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { 1 k }$ may be approximated
quite accurately as follows. Define the vector $\left( \right.$ with components
$\zeta _ { j }$ given by

$$\frac { 1 } { \zeta _ { j } } = \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { 1 j } } + \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { 2 j } } .$$

The approximate covariance matrix $\widetilde { \Sigma }$ is then given in terms of $\left( \right.$
by

$$\widetilde { \Sigma } = \frac { m _ { \cdot } } { m _ { \cdot } - 1 } \left\{ \mathrm { d i a g } \left( \zeta \right) - \zeta \zeta ^ { T } / \zeta _ { \cdot } \right\} .$$
(7.16)

This matrix has rank $k - \quad 1 .$ The simultaneous solution of equations
(7.15) and (7.16) gives the approximate mean and covariance
matrix of $\mathrm { Y }$ as a function of $\psi .$


### 7.4 Some applications involving binary data


#### 7.4.1 Comparison of two binomial probabilities

Suppose that a clinical trial is undertaken to compare the effect
of a new drug or other therapy with the current standard drug or
therapy. Ignoring side-effects and other complications, the response
for each patient is assumed to be simply 'success' or 'failure'.
In order to highlight the differences between the conditional log
likelihood and the unconditional log likelihood, it is assumed that
the observed data are as shown in Table 7.1. For a single stand-
alone experiment, the numbers in this Table are unrealistically
small, except perhaps as the information available at an early
stage in the experiment when few patients have been recruited.
In the context of a large-scale multi-centre clinical trial, however,
Table 7.1 might represent the contribution of one of the smaller

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="263" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.4 APPLICATIONS INVOLVING BINARY DATA" -->


<table>
<caption>Table 7.1 Hypothetical responses in one segment of a clinical trial</caption>
<tr>
<th colspan="4">Response</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<th></th>
<th>Success</th>
<th>Failure</th>
<th>Total</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Treatment</td>
<td>$Y _ { 1 } = 2$</td>
<td>1</td>
<td>$m _ { 1 } = 3$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Control</td>
<td>$Y _ { 2 } = 1$</td>
<td>3</td>
<td>$m _ { 2 } = 4$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Total</td>
<td>$Y _ { \bullet } = 3$</td>
<td>4</td>
<td>$m . = 7$</td>
</tr>
</table>


centres to the study. It is in the latter context that the methods
described here have greatest impact.

We begin with the usual assumption that responses are indepen-
dent and homogeneous within each of the two groups. Allowance
can be made for the differential effect of covariates measured on
individuals, but to introduce such effects at this stage would only
complicate the argument. Strict adherence to protocol, together
with randomization and concealment, are essential to ensure com-
parability, internal homogeneity and independence. With these
assumptions, the numbers of successes in each treatment group
may be regarded as independent binomial variables $Y _ { i } \sim B \left( m _ { i } , \pi _ { i } \right) ,$
where

$$\log \text { it } \pi _ { 1 } = \lambda + \Delta$$
$$\pi _ { 2 } = \lambda .$$
logit
(7.17)

For a single experiment or $2 \times 2$ table, (7.17) is simply a re-paramet-
erization from the original probability scale to the more convenient
logistic scale. Implicit in the re-parameterization, however, is the
assumption that the logistic difference, $\Delta$ is a good and useful
measure of the treatment effect. In particular, when it is required to
pool information gathered at several participating sites or hospitals,
it is often assumed that $\lambda$ may vary from site to site but that $\Delta$
remains constant over all sites regardless of the success rate for the
controls.

In order to set approximate confidence limits for $\Delta ,$ there are
two principal ways in which we may proceed. The simplest way is
to fit the linear logistic model (7.17) using the methods described
in Chapter 4. Approximate confidence limits may be based on $\widehat { \Delta }$
and its large-sample standard error. For the present example this
gives

$$\widehat { \Delta } = \log \left( \frac { 2 \times 3 } { 1 \times 1 } \right) = 1 . 7 9 2 , \quad \mathrm { s . e . } \left( \widehat { \Delta } \right) \simeq 1 . 6 8 3 .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="264" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

Note that the large-sample variance of $\widehat { \Delta }$ is

$\mathrm { v a r } \widehat { \Delta } = 1 / 2 + 1 / 1 + 1 / 1 + 1 / 3 = 1 7 / 6 .$

More accurate intervals are obtained by working with the profile
deviance,

$$D \left( y ; \Delta \right) = 2 l \left( \widehat { \Delta } , \widehat { \lambda } \right) - 2 l \left( \Delta , \widehat { \lambda } _ { \Delta } \right)$$

where $\widehat { \lambda } _ { \Delta }$ is the maximum-likelihood estimate of $\lambda$ for given $\Delta .$
This statistic is easy to compute using standard computer packages.
For the data in Table 7.1, the profile deviance is plotted in Fig. 7.1.
The nominal 90% large-sample confidence interval, determined
graphically, is

$$\left\{ \Delta : D \left( y ; \Delta \right) - D \left( y ; \widehat { \Delta } \right) < 2 . 7 1 \right\} = \left( - 0 . 8 0 , 4 . 9 5 \right) .$$


<figure>
<figcaption>Fig. 7.1 Graphical comparison of hypergeometric and binomial deviance functions for the data in Table 7.1. Nominal 90% intervals for the log odds ratio, $\Delta ,$ are indicated.</figcaption>

The alternative approach advocated here is to eliminate $\lambda$ by
using the conditional likelihood given $Y _ { 0 } .$ The hypergeometric log
likelihood is

$$l _ { c } \left( \Delta \right) = y _ { 1 } \Delta - \log P _ { 0 } \left( e ^ { \Delta } \right) ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="265" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.4 APPLICATIONS INVOLVING BINARY DATA" -->

where, for Table 7.1, $P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right)$ is equal to the cubic polynomial

$$P _ { 0 } \left( \psi \right) = 4 + 1 8 \psi + 1 2 \psi ^ { 2 } + \psi ^ { 3 } .$$

The hypergeometric likelihood has its maximum at a point $\widehat { \Delta } _ { c }$
different from the unconditional maximum $\widehat { \Delta } .$ In general $| \widehat { \Delta } _ { \mathrm { c } } | \leq$
$| \widehat { \Delta } | ,$ with equality only at the origin. More precisely $\widehat { \Delta } _ { \mathrm { c } }$ satisfies
the standard exponential-family condition

$$y _ { 1 } = e ^ { \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } } P _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \left( e ^ { \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } } \right) / P _ { 0 } \left( e ^ { \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } } \right) = E \left( Y _ { 1 } | Y _ { \cdot } ; \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } \right) .$$

In the example under discussion we find

$$\widehat { \Delta } _ { c } = 1 . 4 9 3 , \quad \mathrm { s . e . } \left( \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } \right) \simeq 1 . 4 9 2 ,$$

where the standard error is computed in the usual way, namely

$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } \right) \simeq 1 / \mathrm { v a r } \left( Y ; \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } \right) = 1 / 0 . 4 4 9 5 .$$

The conditional deviance function

$$2 l _ { c } \left( \widehat { \Delta } _ { c } \right) - 2 l _ { c } \left( \Delta \right)$$

is plotted as the solid line in Fig 7.1 and departs markedly from
the profile deviance for large values of $\Delta .$


#### 7.4.2 Combination of information from several $2 \times 2$ tables

Suppose that data in the form of Table 7.1 are available from several
sources, centres or strata, all cooperating in the same investigation.
In the context of a multi-centre clinical trial, the strata are the
medical centres participating in the trial. In some trials there may
be many such centres, each contributing only a small proportion of
the total patients enrolled. At each centre, one would expect that
the pool of patients suitable for inclusion in the trial would differ
in important respects that are difficult to measure. For instance,
pollution levels, water hardness, rainfall, noise levels and other less
tangible variables might have an effect on the response. In addition,
nursing care and staff morale could have an appreciable effect on
patients who are required to remain in hospital. Consequently, one

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="266" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

would expect the success rate for any medical treatment to vary
appreciably from centre to centre.

Consequently, if we write

$$\pi _ { 1 i } = \mathrm { p r } \left( \mathrm { s u c c e s s } | \mathrm { t r e a t m e n t } \right)$$
$$\pi _ { 2 i } = \mathrm { p r } \left( \mathrm { s u c c e s s } | \mathrm { c o n t r o l } \right)$$

for the success probabilities at centre $i ,$ we may consider the linear
logistic model

$$\sin ^ { 2 } 1$$
$$\mathrm { l o g i t } \pi _ { 2 i } = \lambda _ { i } , \quad i = 1 , \ldots , n . ,$$
logit T1i = 1; + 4
(7.18)

The idea behind this parameterization is that $\Delta > 0$ implies that
treatment is uniformly beneficial at all centres regardless of the
control success rate: $\Delta < 0$ implies that the new treatment is
uniformly poorer than the standard procedure. There is, of course,
the possibility that $\Delta$ varies from centre to centre, even to the
extent that $\Delta > 0$ for some centres and $\Delta < 0$ for others. Such
interactions require careful investigation and detailed plausible
explanation.

One obvious difficulty with the linear logistic model (7.18) is
that it contains $n + 1$ parameters to be estimated on the basis of $2 n$
observed binomial proportions. In such circumstances, maximum
likelihood need not be consistent or efficient for large $n .$ However,
following the general argument outlined in section 7.2.2, if we
condition on the observed success totals, $Y _ { . i } ,$ at each of the centres,
we have

$$Y _ { 1 i } | Y _ { . i } \sim H \left( \mathrm { m } _ { i } , y _ { . i } ; \psi \right) .$$
(7.19)

The hypergeometric log likelihood is thus the sum of $n$ conditionally
independent terms and depends on only one parameter, namely
$\psi = e ^ { \Delta } .$ Provided that the total conditional $F$ Fisher information is
sufficiently large, standard large-sample likelihood theory applies
to the conditional likelihood.

The conditional $\log$ likelihood for $\Delta$ is

$$l _ { c } \left( \Delta \right) = \sum _ { i } \left\{ y _ { 1 i } \Delta - \log P _ { 0 } \left( e ^ { \Delta } ; m _ { 1 i } , m _ { 2 i } , y _ { i } \right) \right\} ,$$

where additional arguments have been appended to the polynomial
$P _ { 0 } \left( \cdot \right)$ to emphasize its dependence on the marginal totals for
$\mathrm { s t r a t u m } i .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="267" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.4 APPLICATIONS INVOLVING BINARY DATA" -->

The score statistic for no treatment effect is

$$U = \partial l _ { c } / \partial \Delta | _ { \Delta = 0 } = \sum _ { i } \left\{ Y _ { 1 i } - E \left( Y _ { 1 i } \right) \right\} = \sum _ { i } \left\{ Y _ { 1 i } - m _ { 1 i } y _ { . i } / m _ { . i } \right\} .$$

The exact null variance of $U$ is the sum of hypergeometric variances,
namely

$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( U \right) = \sum _ { i } m _ { 1 i } m _ { 2 i } y _ { i } \left( m _ { \cdot i } - y _ { \cdot i } \right) / \left\{ m _ { \cdot i } ^ { 2 } \left( m _ { \cdot i } - 1 \right) \right\} .$$

The approximate one-sided significance level for the hypothesis of
no treatment effect is $1 - \Phi \left( z ^ { - } \right) ,$ where

$$Z ^ { - } = \left( U - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) / \sigma _ { U }$$

is the continuity-corrected value. This test, first proposed by
Mantel and Haenszel (1959), is known as the Mantel-Haenszel
test. The Mantel-Haenszel estimator, which is different from the
conditional likelihood estimator, is derived in Exercise 9.10.


#### 7.4.3 Example: Ille-et-vilaine study of oesophageal cancer

The data shown in Table 7.2 is a summary of the Ille-et-vilaine
retrospective study of the effect of alcohol consumption on the
incidence of oesophageal cancer. A more complete list of the
data, including information on tobacco consumption, is given in
Appendix 1 of Breslow and Day (1980). In a retrospective study
the numbers of cases (subjects with cancer) and the number of
controls is to be regarded as fixed by the study design. The alcohol
consumption rate $\left( \mathrm { h i g h } / \mathrm { l o w } \right)$ is the effective response. However, for
the reasons given in section 4.4.3, the roles of these two variables
can be reversed. We may, therefore, regard alcohol consumption
rate as the explanatory covariate and outcome (cancer/no cancer)
as the response even though such a view is not in accord with the
sampling scheme. Since the analysis that follows is conditional
on both sets of marginal totals, this role-reversal presents no
conceptual difficulty.

It is common to find that the incidence of cancer increases with
age. The cases in this study are older on average than the controls.
If age were ignored in the analysis, the apparent effect of alcohol

<!-- PageNumber="268" -->
<!-- PageHeader="CONDITIONAL LIKELIHOODS" -->

consumption would be inflated. For that reason it is advisable
to stratify the data by age. In other words, cases are matched
with controls of a similar age. The treatment effect is therefore a
comparison of cancer incidence rates between subjects of similar
age.

Three models are considered.

1\. a model in which the log odds-ratio is zero, meaning that alco-
hol consumption has no effect on the incidence of oesophageal
cancer.

2\. a model in which the log odds-ratio is constant, meaning
that increased alcohol consumption increases the odds for
oesophageal cancer by the factor $e ^ { \psi }$ uniformly over all age
groups.

3\. a model in which the log odds-ratio increases or decreases
linearly with increasing age.

Algebraically, these models may be written in the form

(i) $\log \psi _ { i } = 0 ,$

(ii) $\log \psi _ { i } = \beta _ { 0 } ,$

(iii)
$\log \psi _ { i } = \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } \left( i - 3 . 5 \right) ,$
(7.20)

where $i = 1 , \ldots , 6$ indexes the age strata. The residual deviances
for these three models are 89.83, 10.73 and 10.29 on 6, 5 and 4
degrees of freedom respectively.

<!-- PageNumber="269" -->
<!-- PageHeader="7.4 APPLICATIONS INVOLVING BINARY DATA" -->
