The random effects account for about half of the total variability
in the parameter estimates, although not all contrasts among the
$\widehat { \pi } \mathrm { s }$ are affected equally (Exercise 14.12). The pooled estimate of
the mixed contrast is $\widehat { \pi } _ { \mathrm { R W } } - \widehat { \pi } _ { \mathrm { W R } } = 0 . 3 4 4 5$ with estimated standard
error 0.0904. Thus the evidence for a non-zero mixed contrast is
evidently very strong. From this analysis there is no evidence of
differences among the probabilities $\pi _ { R R } ,$ $\pi _ { \mathrm { R W } }$ and $\pi _ { \mathrm { w w } } .$

Despite the fact that the magnitudes of the random effects as
estimated in Table 14.10 are approximately equal for the two sexes
it appears from closer inspection that the nature of the effect for
males is quite different than for females. A comparison of the
two sets of animal totals for the Summer '86 and the first Fall '86
experiment shows a strong correlation for males but no evidence
of any correlation for females. Thus it appears that the male
random effects persist over several months at least whereas the
female effects are short-lived. This conclusion applies to both male
populations and both female populations.


### 14.6 Bibliographic notes

Linear models in which there is more than one variance component
have been used for many years going back at least to the work of
Fisher and Yates in agricultural experiments. For detailed accounts
of specific designs see Kempthorne (1952, Chapter 9) or Cox (1958c,
Chapters 7,8). For the estimation of variance components more
generally in cases where the design is unbalanced, the method
of restricted maximum likelihood is usually preferred to ordinary
maximum likelihood. For an account of this see section 7.2,
Patterson and Thompson (1971) or Harville (1974, 1977).

There is a parallel but extensive literature on educational test-

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="451" -->
<!-- PageHeader="14.6 BIBLIOGRAPHIC NOTES" -->

ing, which uses ordinary maximum-likelihood estimation for unbal-
anced Normal-theory linear models. See, for example, Goldstein
(1986) or Bock (1989).

Although the need has long been recognized by practising statis-
ticians, the development of analogous models and techniques of
estimation for non-Normal data or for non-linear effects has pro-
ceeded very slowly. Some notable exceptions are Stiratelli, Laird
and Ware (1984), who use a Bayesian argument, with a diffuse prior
on the regression coefficients, for the estimation of the dispersion
parameters. Gilmour, Anderson and Rae (1985) discuss a random-
effects probit model. For Normal-theory linear models the previous
two methods reduce to restricted maximum likelihood. Anderson
and Aitkin (1985) develop an unmodified maximum-likelihood esti-
mation procedure for nested variance components in linear logistic
and probit models. Their likelihood is obtained by direct numeri-
cal integration assuming Normal random effects, and the resulting
estimates are not the same as those suggested here.

In the previous three papers the effects of interest are defined as
linear contrasts of the conditional logits. In section 14.5.3, however,
the effects of interest are defined in terms of the unconditional
probabilities or logits: the random-effects model (14.10) is used
only to justify the choice of covariance matrix. A qualitatively
similar argument is given by Prentice (1988). Zeger, Liang and
Self (1985) and Liang and Zeger (1986) also argue in favour of
specifying models for the unconditional rather than the conditional
probabilities, but the choice must ultimately be governed by what
the interesting parameters are in any given context. For further
discussion regarding the distinction between subject-specific mod-
els such as (14.10) and population-averaged models corresponding
to the estimates (14.12), see Zeger, Liang and Albert (1988).

Morton (1987) considers models for counted data in which
the random effects are nested and effects are multiplicative. His
method of estimation, using quasi-likelihood estimating functions,
is very similar to that used here. The salamander example is rather
unusual in that the random effects are crossed rather than nested.

Despite the apparent paucity of references, this subject is ex-
tremely important with a broad range of applications. At the
time of writing it is evident that the topic is a rich source of good
research problems.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="452" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMPONENTS OF DISPERSION" -->


### 14.7 Further results and exercises 14

14.1 Consider the vector $\mathrm { S }$ whose 44 components are displayed in
Table 14.8. Show that $T _ { 1 } = S _ { 1 } - S _ { R R } / 1 0 - S _ { R W } / 1 0$ is uncorrelated
with $S _ { R R } ,$ $S _ { \mathrm { R W } } ,$ $S _ { \mathrm { w r } }$ and $S _ { \mathrm { w w } } ,$ and that $E \left( T _ { 1 } \right) = 0 .$ Hence deduce
that the quasi-likelihood estimates of the parameters are given by
(14.12).

14.2 Suppose that $\epsilon$ is a Normal random variable with mean zero
and variance $\sigma ^ { 2 } .$ Define the following random variable and its
expectation:

$$P = \frac { \exp \left( \alpha + \epsilon \right) } { 1 + \exp \left( \alpha + \epsilon \right) } ,$$

and T = E(P).

Justify empirically the approximation $\pi \simeq F \left( \alpha ^ { * } \right) ,$ where $F \left( x \right) =$
$e ^ { x } / \left( 1 + e ^ { x } \right) ,$ and

$$\alpha ^ { * } = \alpha - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } \tanh \left\{ \alpha \left( 1 + 2 \exp \left( - \sigma ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) \right) / 6 \right\} .$$

For $\sigma ^ { 2 } < 2 ,$ the maximum error of this approximation is about
0.003 on the probability scale. Show that the approximation has
the correct limiting behaviour in the limit as $\alpha \rightarrow \pm \infty$ for fixed
$\sigma ^ { 2 } .$

By differentiating the above approximation with respect to $\alpha ,$
show that the variance of $P$ is approximately

$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( P \right) \simeq \sigma ^ { 2 } \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right) \pi ^ { \dagger } \left( 1 - \pi ^ { \dagger } \right) \times \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( 1 + 2 \exp \left( - \sigma ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) \right) ,$$

where $\pi ^ { \dagger } = F \left( \alpha \left( 1 + 2 \exp \left( - \sigma ^ { 2 } / 2 \right) \right) / 3 \right) .$ This approximation is
considerably more accurate than the Taylor approximation in
(14.11).

14.3 Suppose that $\epsilon _ { 1 } ,$ $\epsilon _ { 2 }$ are Normal random variables with vari-
ances $\sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ,$ $\sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ,$ and correlation $\rho .$ Show that the correlation $\rho ^ { \prime }$ of
$F \left( \epsilon _ { 1 } \right)$ and $G \left( \epsilon _ { 2 } \right)$ satisfies

$$\frac { | \rho | } { | \rho ^ { \prime } | } \simeq 1 + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left\{ \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { F ^ { \prime \prime } } { F ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { G ^ { \prime \prime } } { G ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 2 } - 2 \rho \sigma _ { 1 } \sigma _ { 2 } \frac { F ^ { \prime \prime } G ^ { \prime \prime } } { F ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } } \right\} \geq 1$$

when terms of order $\sigma ^ { 4 }$ and smaller are ignored. Under what
conditions is $\rho ^ { \prime }$ equal to $\rho$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="453" -->
<!-- PageHeader="14.7 EXERCISES 14" -->

14.4 Using the notation of the previous Exercise, define the ran-
dom variables $P _ { 1 } ,$ $P _ { 2 } ,$ together with their expectations as follows:

$$P _ { 1 } = \frac { \exp \left( \alpha _ { 1 } + \epsilon _ { 1 } \right) } { 1 + \exp \left( \alpha _ { 1 } + \epsilon _ { 1 } \right) } , \quad P _ { 2 } = \frac { \exp \left( \alpha _ { 2 } + \epsilon _ { 2 } \right) } { 1 + \exp \left( \alpha _ { 2 } + \epsilon _ { 2 } \right) } ,$$
$$\pi _ { 1 } = E \left( P _ { 1 } \right) ,$$
$$\pi _ { 2 } = E \left( P _ { 2 } \right) .$$

Using the results given in the previous Exercises, find an approxi-
mation for the covariance of $P _ { 1 }$ and $P _ { 2 } .$

Compute the exact covariance numerically for variances and
covariances in the range 0-2. Comment on the adequacy of the
approximate formula.

14.5 Compute the components of the sufficient statistic S for
the first fall '86 experiment (data in Table 14.5). Compare the
individual animal totals with those in Table 14.8. Compute the
correlation coefficients or regression coefficients of the Fall totals
on the Summer totals for each of the four groups. Comment on
your findings.

14.6 Show that if $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ have common mean $\mu$ and covariance
matrix $\kappa _ { i , j } ,$ then the expected value of

$$s ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { n - 1 } \sum \left( Y _ { i } - \bar { Y } \right) ^ { 2 }$$

is given by

$$E \left( s ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { 1 } { n } \sum _ { i } \kappa _ { i , i } - \frac { 1 } { n \left( n - 1 \right) } \sum _ { i \neq j } \kappa _ { i , j } .$$

14.7 The pooled estimate of the dispersion components in Ta-
ble 14.10 is the average estimate for the three experiments. By
pooling together the sums of squares for $F$ and $M$ from the three
experiments and equating these totals to their expectations, show
that the pooled estimates are $\widetilde { \sigma } _ { \mathrm { r } } ^ { 2 } = 0 . 9 0 3 5$ and $\widetilde { \sigma } _ { \mathrm { m } } ^ { 2 } = 0 . 8 7 5 9 .$

14.8 Suppose that the random vector $\mathrm { Y }$ satisfies the linear model

$$E \left( \mathrm { Y } \right) = \mathrm { X } \beta , \quad \mathrm { c o v } \left( \mathrm { Y } \right) = \mathrm { V } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="454" -->
<!-- PageHeader="COMPONENTS OF DISPERSION" -->

Let $\widetilde { \beta }$ be the ordinary least-squares estimate $\widetilde { \beta } = \left( \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { X } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { Y }$
☒ ☒
and let $\mathrm { R }$ be the residual vector

$$R = \left( I - P \right) Y = Y - X \widetilde { \beta } .$$

Let $\mathcal{X}$ denote the column space of $\mathrm { X } .$ Show that if $\mathrm { V } x$ lies in $\mathcal{X}$
for each $x \in \mathcal{X}$ then $\mathrm { R }$ and $\widetilde { \beta }$ are uncorrelated and

$$\widetilde { \beta } = \widehat { \beta } = \left( \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { W X } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { W Y } ,$$

where $\mathrm { W } = \mathrm { V } ^ { - 1 } .$ Deduce also that $\mathrm { V } ^ { - 1 } x \in \mathcal{X} .$ [Kruskal, 1968].

14.9 Using the result established in the previous exercise show
that the maximum quasi-likelihood estimates of the parameters
in the salamander example are given by (14.12) provided that
the experiment is suitably balanced. Obtain the required balance
condition.

14.10 Show that if $Y$ has the gamma distribution $G \left( \mu , \nu \right) ,$ then

$$\mathrm { v a r } \left\{ \log \left( Y \right) \right\} = \psi ^ { \prime } \left( \nu \right) ,$$

where $\psi \left( x \right) = \Gamma ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) / \Gamma \left( x \right) .$ Under what conditions is the approxi-
mation $\mathrm { v a r } \left( \log Y \right) \simeq \mathrm { c v } ^ { 2 } \left( Y \right)$ adequate for the gamma family?
☒

14.11 Repeat the calculations of the previous exercise under the
assumption that $Y$ has the log-Normal distribution, $\log \left( Y \right) \sim$
$N \left( \mu , \sigma ^ { 2 } \right) .$

14.12 Consider the model formula (14.10) written in matrix
notation in the form

$$Z b + X \pi ,$$

in which $Z$ is a $\left( 1 2 0 \times 4 0 \right)$ incidence matrix identifying the animals
paired together in each trial, and $\mathrm { X }$ is a $\left( 1 2 0 \times 4 \right)$ incidence matrix
for the cross type, $\mathrm { R } / \mathrm { R } ,$ $\mathrm { R } / \mathrm { W } ,$ $\mathrm { W } / \mathrm { R }$ and $\mathrm { w } / \mathrm { W } .$ Show that the
matrix $\left( I - Z \left( Z ^ { T } Z \right) ^ { - } Z ^ { T } \right) X$ has rank 1, with span corresponding to
the contrast $\mathrm { R } / \mathrm { R } - \mathrm { R } / \mathrm { W } - \mathrm { W } / \mathrm { R } + \mathrm { W } / \mathrm { W } .$ Hence justify the claim
made in section 14.5.3 that most of the effects of interest are not
estimable in (14.10).

Using (14.12) and (14.13) find an estimate of the above contrast
and obtain a numerical estimate of the standard error, (i) under
the assumption that the between-animal variances are zero, and
(ii) using the estimates given in Table 14.10. Explain briefly why
the standard error in (ii) is smaller than in (i).

<!-- PageBreak -->


## CHAPTER 15 Further topics


### 15.1 Introduction

This chapter describes briefly a number of topics related to gener-
alized linear models, some of which are of current research interest.


### 15.2 Bias adjustment

In large samples the bias of maximum-likelihood estimators is
$O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) ,$ and hence negligible compared with standard errors. For
samples of more modest size, or for problems in which the number
of parameters is appreciable compared with $n ,$ the bias may not
be entirely negligible. In such cases the usual approximations can
often be improved by making a bias adjustment to the maximum-
likelihood estimate. In what follows we describe how the leading
term in the asymptotic bias can be computed by weighted linear
regression.


#### 15.2.1 Models with canonical link

In the case of full exponential-family models with canonical link
function, such as linear logistic models for binomial data, log-linear
models for Poisson data, inverse linear models for exponential data,
the approximate bias of the maximum-likelihood estimate can be
obtained by a very simple supplementary computation, which we
now describe.

Using tensor notation with implicit summation over indices
that appear twice, the components of the approximate bias vector
$\mathrm { b } = \widetilde { E \left( \widehat { \beta } - \beta \right) }$ are given as follows:

$$E \left( \widehat { \beta } ^ { r } - \beta ^ { r } \right) = b ^ { r } \simeq - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa ^ { r , s } \kappa ^ { t , u } \kappa _ { s , t , u } .$$
(15.1)

<!-- PageNumber="455" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="456" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->

For a derivation of this and related asymptotic formulae for max-
imum likelihood estimators, see McCullagh (1987, Chapter 7). In
this formula $\kappa ^ { r , s }$ are the components of the inverse Fisher informa-
tion matrix, elsewhere written using matrix notation as $\left( X ^ { T } W X \right) ^ { - 1 } ,$
☒

☒
where, for canonical-link models, $W = \mathrm { c o v } \left( Y \right) .$ The expression for
☒
the components of the three-way array $\kappa _ { s , t , u } ,$ in terms of the model
matrix $X = \left\{ x _ { r } ^ { i } \right\}$ is

$$\kappa _ { s , t , u } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } x _ { s } ^ { i } x _ { t } ^ { i } x _ { u } ^ { i } \kappa _ { 3 i } ,$$

where $\kappa _ { 3 i }$ is the third cumulant of the $i$ ith component of the
response vector.

As an intermediate step in the derivation it is helpful to consider
the contracted array with components

$$b _ { s } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \kappa _ { s , t , u } \kappa ^ { t , u } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } \kappa _ { 3 i } x _ { t } ^ { i } x _ { u } ^ { i } \kappa ^ { t , u } .$$
(15.2)

Using matrix notation, the product $x _ { t } ^ { i } x _ { u } ^ { j } \kappa ^ { t , u }$ is written as the $n \times n$
symmetric matrix

$$Q = X \left( X ^ { T } W X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { T } ,$$ ☒

which is the asymptotic covariance matrix of $\widehat { \eta } .$ Consequently the
final factor appearing in (15.2) is the ith diagonal element of $Q ,$
which we write as $Q _ { i i } .$ Thus

$$b _ { s } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } \kappa _ { 2 i } Q _ { i i } \frac { \kappa _ { 3 i } } { \kappa _ { 2 i } } .$$

Using matrix notation the components on the right of the above
equation are just $\mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { W } \xi ,$ where $\xi _ { i } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q _ { i i } \kappa _ { 3 i } / \kappa _ { 2 i } .$
☒

Evidently from (15.2) the bias vector $\mathrm { b }$ is obtained by premulti-
plying the components $b _ { s }$ by the inverse Fisher information matrix.
This operation gives

$$b = \left( X ^ { T } W X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { T } W \xi ,$$ ☒ ☒
(15.3)

which is easily obtained as the vector of regression coefficients in
the formal linear regression of $\xi$ on $X$ with $\mathrm { W }$ as weight vector. In
other words we retain the weights and the model formula from the
log-linear or linear logistic model, but the link function becomes the
identity and response vector becomes $\xi .$ The binomial index vector
and any prior weights are assumed to be incorporated into $\mathrm { w } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="457" -->
<!-- PageHeader="15.2 BIAS ADJUSTMENT" -->


#### 15.2.2 Non-canonical models

For non-canonical models the tensor expression for the first-order
asymptotic bias of $\widehat { \beta }$ is a little more complicated because it involves
the covariance between the vector of first-order derivatives and the
matrix of second-order derivatives of the log-likelihood function.
However, calculations similar to those given in the previous section
show that the bias vector can be obtained by a similar supplemen-
tary regression computation. It is only necessary to re-define the
formal response vector in this regression and then to use (15.3).
We find that the components of $\xi$ are given by

$$\xi _ { i } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \right) Q _ { i i } ,$$
(15.4)

where $\mu _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \partial \mu _ { i } / \partial \eta _ { i }$ and $\mu _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } = \partial ^ { 2 } \mu _ { i } / \partial \eta _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ are the derivatives of the
inverse link function. Note that the weights in (15.3) are the usual
quadratic weights, namely $W _ { i } = \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 } / \kappa _ { 2 i } .$

The following Table gives expressions for $\xi _ { i }$ for some common
link functions.


<table>
<tr>
<th>Link</th>
<th>$\xi _ { i }$</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>identity</td>
<td>0</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\log$</td>
<td>$- Q _ { i i } / 2$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>logit</td>
<td>$Q _ { i i } \left( \pi _ { i } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>probit</td>
<td>$Q _ { i i } \eta _ { i } / 2$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$c - \log - \log$</td>
<td>$Q _ { i i } \left( \exp \left( \eta _ { i } \right) - 1 \right) / 2$</td>
</tr>
</table>


For binary regression models $\xi _ { i }$ has the same sign as $\eta _ { i } ,$ though
the vectors $\xi$ and $\eta$ are not co-linear in $R ^ { n } .$ However, under
conditions of approximate quadratic balance $\left( Q _ { i i } = \text { const } \right) ,$ and
provided that $| \beta |$ is small, it may be shown that the bias vector $\mathrm { b }$
and the parameter vector $\beta$ are approximately co-linear. A very
rough approximation for small $| \beta |$ is

$$\mathrm { b } \simeq p \beta / m _ { . } ,$$
(15.5)

where $m _ { - } = \sum m _ { i }$ and $p = \dim \left( \beta \right) .$ Thus bias adjustment for
binary regression models has an effect on the parameter estimates
approximately the same as shrinkage towards the origin by the
factor $1 \quad - \quad p / m . .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="458" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->


#### 15.2.3 Example: Lizard data (continued)

To illustrate these computations we use the linear logistic model
(4.24) applied to the data in Table 4.5. The parameter estimates
shown in Table 4.8 lead to the following fitted quantities:

$$\widehat { \pi } = \left( 0 . 8 7 4 9 , 0 . 8 9 7 7 , 0 . 7 6 9 9 , 0 . 9 5 5 8 , 0 . 9 6 4 5 , 0 . 9 1 2 0 , \ldots \right) ,$$
$$\widehat { Q } _ { i i } = \left( 0 . 1 1 6 1 , 0 . 1 3 3 3 , 0 . 1 2 4 6 , 0 . 1 5 0 6 , 0 . 1 7 4 9 , 0 . 1 5 3 0 , \ldots \right) ,$$
$$\widehat { \xi } _ { i } = \left( 0 . 0 4 3 5 , 0 . 0 5 3 0 , 0 . 0 3 3 6 , 0 . 0 6 8 7 , 0 . 0 8 1 2 , 0 . 0 6 3 0 , \ldots \right) ,$$
$$\widehat { w } _ { i } = \left( 2 . 4 0 8 5 , 0 . 8 2 6 6 , 1 . 4 1 7 1 , 0 . 5 4 8 8 , 0 . 2 7 4 0 , 0 . 9 6 3 4 , \ldots \right) .$$

Only the first six components of the fitted vectors are shown here:
these correspond to the first two rows of Table 4.5. Note that $\widehat { w } _ { i }$
for linear logistic models is just $m _ { i } \widehat { \pi } _ { i } \left( 1 - \widehat { \pi } _ { i } \right) .$

Weighted linear regression of $\widehat { \xi } ,$ using the same model formula,
gives the bias vector $\widehat { \mathrm { b } }$ shown together with $\widehat { \beta }$ in the following
Table:

<th>$\widehat { \beta } - \widehat { \mathrm { b } }$</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mu$</td>
<td>1.9447</td>
<td>0.3408</td>
<td>0.0436</td>
<td>1.9011</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$H$</td>
<td>1.1300</td>

<td>-0.7626</td>

The largest biases here are about 10% of a standard error. In cases
of marginal statistical significance biases of this magnitude could
have a small effect on the conclusions, but they are unlikely to be of
any consequence in this example. However an examination of the
approximate biases is helpful here as a check on the significance of
the factor $S ,$ which, though significant in the maximum-likelihood
analysis, does not show up as significant in the preliminary analysis
in Table 4.6 and Fig. 4.2. The bias adjustment indicated above
reduces the significance of $S ,$ but not by an amount sufficient to
alter the conclusions.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="459" -->
<!-- PageHeader="15.3 COMPUTATION OF BARTLETT ADJUSTMENTS" -->


### 15.3 Computation of Bartlett adjustments


#### 15.3.1 General theory

A simple, or fully specified, null hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ may be
tested using the likelihood-ratio statistic, which is twice the differ-
ence between the maximum log likelihood and the value attained
at $\theta _ { 0 } .$ For generalized linear models in which the dispersion par-
ameter is known, this difference may be written in terms of the
deviance as follows:

$$\Lambda = 2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } ; Y \right) - 2 l \left( \theta _ { 0 } ; Y \right) = D \left( Y ; \theta _ { 0 } \right) - D \left( Y ; \widehat { \theta } \right) .$$

We have assumed here for simplicity of notation that the dispersion
parameter is equal to unity.

Under the usual asymptotic regularity conditions for large sam-
ples the asymptotic mean of this statistic is

$$E \left\{ D \left( Y ; \theta _ { 0 } \right) - D \left( Y ; \widehat { \theta } \right) \right\} = p + \epsilon _ { p } + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) ,$$
$$= p \left\{ 1 + b _ { p } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right\} + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) ,$$

where $p = \dim \left( \theta \right)$ and $b \left( \theta \right)$ is known as the Bartlett adjustment
factor. In fact it is possible to show that all cumulants up to any
fixed order, $r$ r., are given to the same order of approximation by

$$\kappa _ { r } \left\{ D \left( Y ; \theta _ { 0 } \right) - D \left( Y ; \widehat { \theta } \right) \right\} = \left( r - 1 \right) ! 2 ^ { r - 1 } p \left\{ 1 + b _ { p } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right\} ^ { r } + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right.$$
(15.6)

For an outline proof in the single-parameter case, see Appendix $\mathrm { C } .$
The leading term in this expression is just the $r$ rth cumulant of the
$\chi _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ distribution. From the multiplicative property of cumulants
it can be seen immediately that the cumulants of the adjusted
statistic

$$\Lambda ^ { \prime } = \frac { \Lambda } { 1 + b _ { p } }$$

agree with those of the $x _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ distribution when terms of order $O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right)$
are ignored. Note that $b _ { p }$ and $\epsilon _ { p }$ are both $O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right)$ by assumption.

Although convergence of the cumulants implies convergence
in distribution provided that the asymptotic cumulants uniquely
determine a distribution, the order of magnitude of the discrepancy
in the cumulants is not necessarily the same as the size of the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="460" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->

error in the cumulative distribution function. Nevertheless it seems
plausible to conclude that the distribution of the adjusted statistic
is given by

$$\Lambda ^ { \prime } \sim \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) ,$$

and in fact this claim is correct at least in the non-lattice case. In
the lattice case the error cannot be reduced below $O \left( n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$ without
resorting to discontinuous approximations, which are extremely
inconvenient. It is unclear in that case whether the adjustment
improves the approximation or not.

For composite null hypotheses, which are more common in
applications, $a$ similar adjustment can be made. The statistic can
be written in the form

$$\Lambda \left( \widehat { \theta } , \widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } \right) = 2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } ; Y \right) - 2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } ; Y \right) = D \left( Y ; \widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } \right) - D \left( Y ; \widehat { \theta } \right) ,$$
(15.7)

where $\widehat { \theta } _ { 0 }$ is the estimate of the nuisance parameters under $H _ { 0 } ,$ and
$\widehat { \theta }$ is the unrestricted estimate. Assuming that the hypotheses are
nested, and that $q < p$ is the dimension of the parameter space
under $H _ { 0 } ,$ the mean of this statistic is

$$E \left\{ \Lambda \left( \widehat { \theta } , \widehat { \theta } _ { 0 } \right) \right\} = p + \epsilon _ { p } + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) - \left( q + \epsilon _ { q } + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) \right)$$
$$= p - q + \left( \epsilon _ { p } - \epsilon _ { q } \right) + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right)$$
$$= \left( p - q \right) \left\{ 1 + b _ { p q } \left( \theta \right) \right\} + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) .$$

Thus the required adjustment factor is now

$$b _ { p q } = \left( p b _ { p } - q b _ { q } \right) / \left( p - q \right) = \left( \epsilon _ { p } - \epsilon _ { q } \right) / \left( p - q \right) .$$
(15.8)

The cumulants of the maximized likelihood-ratio statistic (15.7)
obey (15.6) with $p$ replaced by $p \quad - \quad q .$ Hence the cumulants of the
adjusted statistic agree with those of $x _ { p - q } ^ { 2 } .$ apart from terms of
order $O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) .$


#### 15.3.2 Computation of the adjustment

We focus here on computing $\epsilon _ { p }$ as if the null hypothesis were simple.
Differencing is necessary if nuisance parameters are present. All
quantities are computed at $\widehat { \theta } _ { 0 }$ rather than the true $\theta ,$ which is
usually unknown.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="461" -->
<!-- PageHeader="15.3 COMPUTATION OF BARTLETT ADJUSTMENTS" -->

The calculations that follow use the general expression (29)
of McCullagh and Cox (1986). The aim here is to present that
expression in a more readily computable form, by exploiting special
properties of generalized linear models. Final expressions are
presented in matrix notation although intermediate calculations
make some use of index notation.

For the present discussion it is convenient to introduce the
following diagonal matrices.

$$D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime } \right\} = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ d \mu _ { i } / d \eta _ { i } \right\} ,$$
$$D ^ { \left( 2 \right) } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } - \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } d \log V _ { i } / d \mu _ { i } \right\} ,$$
$$W = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } / V _ { i } \right\} = D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } V ^ { - 1 } D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } .$$

In addition to these, the following non-diagonal matrices arise
naturally as a by-product of the weighted least squares algorithm
used to compute parameter estimates.

$$Q = X \left( X ^ { T } W X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { T } ,$$
$$P = D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } Q D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } V ^ { - 1 } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒

Note that $\sigma ^ { 2 } Q$ is the asymptotic covariance matrix of $\widehat { \eta }$ and
$\sigma ^ { 2 } D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } Q D ^ { \left( 1 \right) }$ is the asymptotic covariance matrix of $\widehat { \mu } .$

The first and second derivatives of the log likelihood with respect
to the regression parameters $\beta _ { r } ,$ are

$$U _ { r } = \partial l / \partial \beta _ { r } = \sum _ { i } x _ { r } ^ { i } D _ { i } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y _ { i } - \mu _ { i } \right) ,$$
$$U _ { r s } = \partial ^ { 2 } l / \partial \beta _ { r } \partial \beta _ { s } = \sum _ { i } x _ { r } ^ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } D _ { i } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y _ { i } - \mu _ { i } \right) - x _ { r } ^ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } W _ { i } ,$$

where $x _ { r } ^ { i }$ are the components of the model matrix $\mathrm { X } .$ The
Fisher information matrix is $\mathrm { X } ^ { T } \mathrm { W X } ,$ which we write as $\kappa _ { r , s } ,$ with
☒
inverse $\kappa ^ { r , s } .$

Evidently, for all generalized linear models, the log likelihood
derivatives are linear functions of $Y ,$ a property that simplifies
much of the subsequent calculations. A key step in deriving a
simple expression for the Bartlett factor involves working with the
residual second derivative matrix rather than $U _ { r s } .$ The covariance
of $U _ { r s }$ and $U _ { t }$ is a three-way array whose components are

$$\kappa _ { r s , t } = \sum _ { i } x _ { r } ^ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } D _ { i } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } D _ { i } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } x _ { t } ^ { i } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="462" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->

Since only arithmetic multiplication is involved, the order of terms
in the above sum is immaterial. It is a remarkable property of
generalized linear models that all such 'mixed' cumulants are sym-
metric under index permutation. In other words, for generalized
linear models, but not in general, $\kappa _ { r s , t } = \kappa _ { r t , s } = \kappa _ { s t , r } ,$ and so on.

The residual matrix of second derivatives after linear regression
on $U _ { r }$ is

$$V _ { r s } = \sum _ { i } x _ { r } ^ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } D _ { i } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left\{ \delta _ { i j } - P _ { i j } \right\} \left( Y _ { j } - \mu _ { j } \right) .$$

For purposes of computation we may write

$$V _ { r s } \simeq \sum _ { i } x _ { r } ^ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } D _ { i } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( Y _ { i } - \widehat { \mu } _ { i } \right) ,$$

but the former expression is simpler for theoretical calculations.

The Bartlett factor can now be given as a linear combination of
six invariant functions of the joint cumulants of $U _ { r } ,$ and $V _ { r s } .$ The
invariant fourth cumulant of $U _ { r } ,$ is

$$\rho _ { 4 } = \sum _ { i } x _ { r } ^ { i } x _ { s } ^ { i } x _ { t } ^ { i } x _ { u } ^ { i } u _ { i } ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 4 } V _ { i } ^ { - 4 } \kappa _ { 4 i } \kappa ^ { r , s } \kappa ^ { t , u } ,$$
$$= \sum _ { i } \left( P _ { i i } \right) ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 4 i } ,$$

(a)

where $P _ { i i }$ are the diagonal elements of the asymmetric projection
matrix $\mathrm { P } ,$ and $\rho _ { 4 i } = \kappa _ { 4 i } / \kappa _ { 2 i } ^ { 2 } ,$ is the usual standardized fourth
cumulant of $Y _ { i } .$ The two quadratic skewness scalars are

$$\rho _ { 1 3 } ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i j } x _ { r } ^ { i } Q _ { i i } \mu _ { i } ^ { \prime 3 } V _ { i } ^ { - 3 } \kappa _ { 3 i } \kappa ^ { r , s } x _ { r } ^ { j } Q _ { j j } \mu _ { j } ^ { \prime 3 } V _ { j } ^ { - 3 } \kappa _ { 3 j }$$

$$= \sum _ { i j } \left( P _ { i i } \kappa _ { 3 i } / \kappa _ { 2 i } \right) V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } P _ { i j } \left( P _ { j j } \kappa _ { 3 j } / \kappa _ { 2 j } \right)$$
$$\rho _ { 2 3 } ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i j } \left( V _ { i } ^ { - 1 } P _ { i j } \right) ^ { 3 } \kappa _ { 3 i } \kappa _ { 3 j } .$$
(b)
(c)

These scalars can be computed easily using simple matrix opera-
tions.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="463" -->
<!-- PageHeader="15.3 COMPUTATION OF BARTLETT ADJUSTMENTS" -->

Similar calculations show that the two scalar measures of the
variability of $V _ { r s } ,$ can be simplified as follows.

$$\nu _ { r s , t u } \kappa ^ { r , s } \kappa ^ { t , u } = q ^ { T } \mathrm { D } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } \mathrm { V } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { I } - \mathrm { P } \right) \mathrm { D } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } q ,$$
$$\nu _ { r s , t u } \kappa ^ { r , t } \kappa ^ { s , u } = \sum _ { i j } Q _ { i j } Q _ { i j } \left[ \mathrm { D } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } \mathrm { V } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { I } - \mathrm { P } \right) \mathrm { D } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } \right] _ { i j } ,$$
(d)
(e)

where $q$ is a vector with components $Q _ { i i } .$ Finally we have one
further scalar

$$\nu _ { r , s , t u } \kappa ^ { r , s } \kappa ^ { t , u } = q ^ { T } \mathrm { D } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } \mathrm { V } ^ { - 1 } \left( \mathrm { I } - \mathrm { P } \right) q ^ { * } ,$$
(f)

where $q _ { j } ^ { * } = q _ { j } W _ { j } \kappa _ { 3 j } / \kappa _ { 2 j } .$ The notation on the left of the preceding
three equations is taken from McCullagh and Cox (1986).

Although the tensor expressions on the left of $\left( d \right) - \left( f \right)$ are
algebraically more appealing than the matrix formulae, the latter
expressions have the advantage for numerical purposes that they
use only simple operations on matrices and vectors. Numerical
computation involving higher-order arrays is thereby avoided.

In terms of these scalars the correction may be written as the
linear combination

$$\epsilon _ { p } = - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( a \right) + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( b \right) + \frac { 1 } { 6 } \left( c \right) - \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left( d \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( e \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( f \right) .$$
(15.9)

If the canonical link is used only the first three of these terms
contribute.


#### 15.3.3 Example: exponential regression model

Suppose that $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are independent exponential random vari-
ables with $\eta _ { i } = \log \mu _ { i }$ satisfying the linear model $\eta = X \beta .$ We find
that the required matrices are as follows:

$$D ^ { \left( 1 \right) } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ \mu _ { i } \right\} , \quad D ^ { \left( 2 \right) } = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ - \mu _ { i } \right\} ,$$

W = I,

$$Q = X \left( X ^ { T } X \right) ^ { - 1 } X ^ { T } , \quad P = \mathrm { d i a g } \left\{ \mu _ { i } \right\} Q d i a g \left\{ \mu _ { i } ^ { - 1 } \right\} ,$$

so that $P _ { i i } = Q _ { i i }$ even though $P$ is not symmetrical. These
properties permit simplification of (15.9) giving

$$\epsilon _ { p } = \frac { 1 } { 6 } \sum Q _ { i j } ^ { 3 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } q ^ { T } \left( I - Q \right) q .$$
(15.10)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="464" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->

In this particular case, since the model is of the translation type,
$\epsilon _ { p }$ does not depend on the value of the parameter.

To take a simple numerical example, consider the data on
survival times for 17 leukaemia patients given by Feigl and Zelen
(1965). The data are discussed by Cox and Snell (1981, pp.148-
150), who consider the model

$$\log \mu _ { i } = \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 1 } x _ { i }$$
(15.11)

in which $\mu _ { i }$ is the expected survival time, and $x _ { i }$ is the logarithm of
the initial white blood cell count. The likelihood-ratio statistic for
testing the hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \beta _ { 1 } = 0$ comes to 6.826 on one degree
of freedom yielding a p-value of 0.89%. Here we compute the
Bartlett-adjusted statistic as a check on the adequacy of the $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$
approximation.

Since in this case

$$Q _ { i j } = \frac { 1 } { n } + \frac { \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) \left( x _ { j } - \bar { x } \right) } { \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } }$$

it follows that

$$\sum _ { i j } Q _ { i j } ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 } { n } + \frac { \left( \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 3 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { \left( \sum \left( x _ { i } - \bar { x } \right) ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } }$$

For the leukaemia data this gives

$$\epsilon _ { 2 } = \left( 4 / 1 7 + 0 . 0 0 0 0 3 8 4 9 \right) / 6 - 0 . 0 6 8 8 / 4 = 0 . 0 2 2 0 .$$

Similar calculations under $H _ { 0 }$ give $\epsilon _ { 1 } = 1 / \left( 6 n \right) = 0 . 0 0 9 8 0 .$ Thus
$b _ { 2 1 } = 0 . 0 1 2 2$ giving a corrected statistic of 6.744. The p-value is
increased to 0.94%-a trivial increase in this case. The usual $x ^ { 2 }$
approximation appears to be quite accurate here even though the
sample size is not especially large.

On the other hand if we were interested in testing the adequacy
of the model (15.11) with no specific alternative in mind, the
likelihood-ratio statistic is equal to 19.46 on 15 degrees of freedom.
For the Bartlett adjustment we find $\epsilon _ { 1 7 } = 1 7 / 6$ giving

$$b _ { 1 7 , 2 } = \left( 1 7 / 6 - 0 . 0 2 2 0 \right) / 1 5 = 0 . 1 8 7 .$$

Thus the adjusted statistic is $1 9 . 4 6 / 1 . 1 8 7 = 1 6 . 3 9 .$ The p-value is
thereby increased from 19.4% to 35.6%. The 19% reduction in the
value of the statistic is substantial and could in principle weaken
the force of the conclusions, although that has not happened in
this case. For example if the unadjusted statistic corresponds to a
p-value of 1%, the p-value for the adjusted statistic is 4%.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="465" -->
<!-- PageHeader="15.4 GENERALIZED ADDITIVE MODELS" -->


### 15.4 Generalized additive models

In choosing the set of terms to be included in the linear predictor
of a generalized linear model we make the prior assumption that
some subset of the terms chosen will give an adequate picture of the
response surface generated by the covariates. If the actual shape
of the surface is not expressible as a function of the terms and link
function chosen then the fitted values will deviate systematically
from the actual response surface. Model-checking techniques de-
scribed in Chapter 12 can be used to detect this state of affairs,
and perhaps to remedy the misfit. An alternative method, due to
Hastie and Tibshirani (1986, 1987ab), is to fit generalized addi-
tive models, which, for continuous covariates $x ,$ replace the linear
predictor $\eta = \sum _ { j } x _ { j } \beta _ { j }$ by the additive model

$$\eta = \alpha + \sum _ { j } f _ { j } \left( x _ { j } \right)$$

in which $f _ { j } \left( x _ { j } \right)$ are smooth functions estimated from the data. The
model remains additive with respect to the covariates, but it is no
longer linear in them. The functions $f _ { j } \left( \cdot \right)$ are identifiable up to an
arbitrary constant, much like the levels of a factor.


#### 15.4.1 Algorithms for fitting

We consider first the fitting procedure for a single covariate $x$ x, using
a generalized additive model with link function $g \left( \cdot \right)$ and variance
function $V \left( \cdot \right) .$ One method associates with each point $\left( y _ { i } , x _ { i } \right)$ a set
of neighbours on the x-axis, and estimates a value of the response
function at $x _ { i }$ for each $i$ by applying the standard GLM algorithm
with linear predictor $\alpha + \beta x _ { i }$ in that neighbourhood. Then $\widehat { f } \left( x _ { i } \right)$ is
the fitted value of $\eta - \alpha$ at $x _ { i } ,$ i.e. we use linear interpolation within
each neighbourhood. This algorithm, also called the local-scoring
algorithm, can be thought of as using a weighted running-lines
smoother on the adjusted dependent variable $z$ z. The algorithm is
efficient because the running-lines smoother can be updated easily
as we pass from one neighbourhood to the next. Neighbourhoods
are usually defined to include a certain fraction, or span, of the
points. Commonly, spans of 40-50% are used, with appropriate
contraction at the ends. The fit obtained depends on the span
used: the shorter the span the rougher the fit.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="466" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->

When more than one covariate is involved the algorithm acquires
an additional loop in which each $f _ { j } \left( \cdot \right)$ is fitted using the current
estimates of the remaining functions. This is known as the back-
fitting algorithm, and takes the form

for $j = 1 , \ldots , p$

form partial residual $r _ { j } = z - \widehat { \eta } + \widehat { f } _ { j } \left( x _ { j } \right)$
smooth $r _ { j }$ with GLM weights $W$ to update $\widehat { f } _ { j } \left( x _ { j } \right)$
repeat.

The full algorithm may be set out as follows:

Initialize: $f _ { j } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( x _ { j } \right) = 0 ,$ $\widehat { \alpha } _ { 0 } = g \left( \bar { y } \right)$

for $i = 0 , 1 , \ldots .$

form current estimates of $\widehat { \eta } ^ { \left( i \right) } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { j } \right) ,$ $\widehat { \mu } ^ { \left( i \right) } ,$ $x ^ { \left( i \right) }$ and $W ^ { \left( i \right) }$

perform back-fitting algorithm to obtain $\widehat { \alpha } ^ { \left( i + 1 \right) }$ and $\widehat { f } _ { j } ^ { \left( i + 1 \right) } \left( x _ { j } \right)$
repeat until deviance stabilizes.

This algorithm depends on the choice of span or fraction of points
used for the local linear fit. At one extreme, if the span is 1, the
algorithm produces the generalized linear fit. At the other extreme,
if the span is equal to $1 / n$ and if all the x-values are distinct then
$\widehat { \mu } _ { i } = y _ { i } ,$ and no smoothing occurs.


#### 15.4.2 Smoothing methods

In the algorithm just described the running-lines smoother may be
replaced by any other smoother, which may in turn be linear or
non-linear. Running-lines and cubic-spline smoothers are linear in
$y ,$ while a running-median smoother is non-linear. Another non-
linear method involves maximizing the local likelihood for each
neighbourhood. In practice, however, this usually gives results very
close to that produced by the linear local scoring method.

The choice of span can be made data-dependent by choosing it
to minimize the cross-validation deviance.

The effective number of parameters associated with an estimated
smooth function can be calculated from the formula

$$\mathrm { t r } \left( 2 \mathrm { S } - \mathrm { S } ^ { T } \mathrm { W S W } ^ { - 1 } \right)$$

in which $\mathrm { S }$ is the smoothing matrix applied to the vector $z$ to
produce $f \left( \cdot \right) ,$ and $\mathrm { W }$ is the weight matrix. For the running-lines
smoother this formula simplifies to $\mathrm { t r } \left( \mathrm { S } \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="467" -->
<!-- PageHeader="15.6 EXERCISES 15" -->


#### 15.4.3 Conclusions

Generalized additive models can be used either as a descriptive
tool for expressing the joint effect of several explanatory variables
as the sum of functions of them individually, or as an exploratory
device to suggest a suitable class of transformations of covariates
to be included in a generalized linear model. The restriction to
additive terms can be relaxed by including product terms of the
form $f _ { 1 2 } \left( x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } \right)$ in addition to $f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } \right)$ and $f _ { 2 } \left( x _ { 2 } \right) .$ However not all
functions of two variables are expressible in the form

$$f _ { 1 } \left( x _ { 1 } \right) + f _ { 2 } \left( x _ { 2 } \right) + f _ { 1 2 } \left( x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } \right) .$$

Generalized partially additive models, in which some covariates
enter linearly and others additively, may eventually prove to be
the most useful application of these techniques.


### 15.5 Bibliographic notes

Bartlett (1937, 1954) gave explicit correction factors for a number
of likelihood-ratio test statistics, including a number of test statis-
tics that arise in multivariate analysis. Similar expressions for log-
linear models were given by Williams (1976), and, for generalized
linear models, by Cordeiro (1983, 1987). See also Ross (1987).

The matrix formulae in section 15.3 appear to be new. An
alternative scheme for computing Bartlett adjustments is described
by Barndorff-Nielsen and Blaesild (1986).


### 15.6 Further results and exercises 15

15.1 Compute the angle in $R ^ { p }$ between the parameter vector
$\widehat { \beta }$ and the bias vector $\widehat { \mathrm { b } }$ for the main-effects model fitted to the
lizard data in section 15.2.3. Use the Fisher information as the
inner product matrix. Comment briefly on the adequacy of the
approximation (15.5).

15.2 Show that the matrix $P$ defined in section 15.3.2 is a
projection matrix. Describe the range space of $P ,$ i.e. the set of
vectors $x$ such that $P x = x .$ Explain how this space depends on
the value of the parameter vector.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="468" -->
<!-- PageHeader="FURTHER TOPICS" -->

15.3 Derive expression (15.10) for the Bartlett adjustment for
exponential regression models from the general expression (15.9).
Show that the second term in (15.10) vanishes if $X$ is the design
matrix for a one-way layout.

15.4 Suppose $Y _ { i } \sim \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } \chi _ { j _ { i } } ^ { 2 } / f _ { i } ,$ independently for $i = 1 , \ldots , k .$
Specify the null hypothesis $H _ { 0 } : \sigma _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \sigma ^ { 2 }$ and the unrestricted
alternative as generalized linear models. Use (15.9) or (15.10) to
compute the Bartlett adjustment factor. [Bartlett, 1937].

15.5 Fit the log-linear model (15.11) to the leukaemia data and
check the calculations given in section 15.3.3.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## APPENDIX A Elementary likelihood theory


### Scalar parameter

This appendix contains a concise summary, without proofs and
omitting esoteric details of regularity conditions, of the more im-
portant properties of likelihood functions, derivatives and esti-
mates, that are used throughout the book.

Definition: The log likelihood is the logarithm of the joint proba-
bility or probability density function of the data, denoted by

$$l \left( \theta ; y \right) = \log f _ { Y } \left( y ; \theta \right) .$$

If $Y$ is a vector having $n$ independent components, the log likeli-
hood is a sum of $n$ independent terms

$$l \left( \theta ; y \right) = \sum \log f _ { Y _ { i } } \left( y _ { i } ; \theta \right) .$$

This representation is often used, at least implicitly, in the deriva-
tion of asymptotic results for large $n$ n.

Derivatives: Under mild regularity conditions the log-likelihood
derivatives satisfy the following moment identities:

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial l } { \partial \theta } \right) = 0$$
$$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } \right) + \mathrm { v a r } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial l } { \partial \theta } \right) = 0$$

(A.1)

The notation above is chosen to emphasize the fact that the twin
processes of differentiation and averaging take place at the same
value of $\theta .$ These relations are obtained by differentiating with
respect to $\theta$ the identity

$$\int f _ { Y } \left( y ; \theta \right) d y \equiv 1 .$$

<!-- PageNumber="469" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="470" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ELEMENTARY LIKELIHOOD THEORY" -->

The necessary regularity conditions are those required to justify
interchanging the order of differentiation with respect to the par-
ameter and integration over the sample space. In particular, the
sample space must be the same for all values of the parameter, or
at least for all $\theta$ in an open neighbourhood of the true parameter
point.

Further differentiation with respect to $\theta$ gives higher-order iden-
tities, sometimes called the Bartlett identities after Bartlett (1954).
The third-order identity is

$$E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 3 } l } { \partial \theta ^ { 3 } } \right) + 3 \mathrm { c o v } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } , \frac { \partial l } { \partial \theta } \right) + E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial l } { \partial \theta } \right) ^ { 3 } = 0$$
(A.2)

These results, connecting moments of log-likelihood derivatives, are
exact for all sample sizes provided, of course, that all the necessary
moments are finite.

Terminology: The log-likelihood derivative $U \left( \theta ; y \right) = \partial l / \partial \theta$ is
sometimes called the score statistic. Its variance

$$i \left( \theta \right) = \mathrm { v a r } _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial l } { \partial \theta } \right) = - E _ { \theta } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } l } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } \right)$$

is called the Fisher information for $\theta$ and plays an important role
in much of what follows.

If the components of $Y$ are independent we may write

$$U \left( \theta ; y \right) = \sum \frac { \partial \log f _ { Y _ { i } } \left( y _ { i } ; \theta \right) } { \partial \theta }$$
$$i \left( \theta \right) = \sum E \left( \frac { \partial \log f _ { Y _ { i } } \left( y _ { i } ; \theta \right) } { \partial \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } ,$$

showing explicitly that the score statistic is a sum of $n$ independent
contributions and that the Fisher information based on the vector
$Y$ is the sum of the Fisher informations from the components.

Asymptotic results: The following results hold under further reg-
ularity conditions related to the behaviour of the sequence of ob-
servations for large $n ,$ or to be more precise, as the amount of
information, $i \left( \theta \right) ,$ becomes large. In particular, the first derivative
suitably normalized converges in distribution to a standard Normal
random variable. Thus

$$i \left( \theta \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \left( \frac { \partial l } { \partial \theta } \right) \sim N \left( 0 , 1 \right) + O _ { p } \left( n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$$
(A.3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="471" -->
<!-- PageHeader="APPENDIX A" -->

provided that the assumed model is correct and that the derivative
is computed at the true parameter point.

The error term is governed more by the magnitude of $i \left( \theta \right)$ than
by the number of components of $Y .$ Most commonly, however, $i \left( \theta \right)$
is roughly proportional to $n$ and it is then immaterial whether we
normalize by $n$ or by $i \left( \theta \right) .$

Maximum-likelihood estimation: Ordinarily the likelihood function
has a single maximum in the interior of the parameter space. The
maximum-likelihood estimate, denoted by $\widehat { \theta } ,$ is then obtained as the
solution of the equation $U \left( \widehat { \theta } ; y \right) = 0 .$ For large $i \left( \theta \right) ,$ the distribution
of $\widehat { \theta }$ is often adequately approximated by

$$\widehat { \theta } - \theta \sim N \left( 0 , i \left( \theta \right) ^ { - 1 } \right) ,$$
(A.4)

assuming, as always, that the model is correct. Higher-order
approximations based on Edgeworth series are given by McCullagh
(1987, p.210).

Occasionally, however, it may be found that the maximum
occurs at a boundary point of the parameter space, which may be
finite or infinite. The above approximation is then inappropriate.
Approximate confidence limits can be obtained directly from the
likelihood function or the likelihood-ratio statistic.

Likelihood-ratio statistics: For large $n ,$ the $\log$ likelihood at $\widehat { \theta } \mathrm { d i f f e r s }$
from the log likelihood at the true parameter point by a random
amount whose approximate distribution is given by

$$2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } ; Y \right) - 2 l \left( \theta ; Y \right) \sim \chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$
(A.5)

This approximation is often quite accurate for small values of $n$
even when the Normal approximation $\left( A . 4 \right)$ is unsatisfactory. The
set of all $\theta$ 0-values satisfying

$$2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } ; y \right) - 2 l \left( \theta , y \right) \leq \chi _ { 1 , \alpha } ^ { 2 }$$

is an approximate $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ confidence set for the parameter
and is usually more accurate in terms of coverage probability than
intervals based on $\left( A . 4 \right) .$

The above approximations can be improved further using meth-
ods given in Appendix $\mathrm { C } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="472" -->
<!-- PageHeader="ELEMENTARY LIKELIHOOD THEORY" -->


### Vector parameter

For vector-valued parameters, the same results apply with suitable
minor changes of notation. The score statistic is the gradient vector
of the log likelihood at $\theta$ and the Fisher information $i \left( \theta \right)$ is now to
be interpreted as a matrix. With obvious modifications, identities
$\left( A . 1 \right)$ apply to the vector case. The vector version of $\left( A . 2 \right)$ is given
by McCullagh (1987, p.202).

The asymptotic results $\left( A . 3 \right)$ and $\left( A . 4 \right)$ extend readily to vector-
valued parameters provided that the limit $i \left( \theta \right) \rightarrow \infty$ is understood
to apply to the eigenvalues of the information matrix and not to
the components. An important regularity condition is that $i \left( \theta \right)$
have constant rank for all $\theta$ in the region of interest.

Nuisance parameters: Suppose that $\theta$ is partitioned into two
components $\theta = \left( \psi , \lambda \right) ,$ both of which may be vector-valued. The
first component is to be regarded as the parameter of interest. The
joint Fisher information matrix for $\theta$ may then be partitioned as
follows:

$$i \left( \theta \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} i _ { \psi \psi } & i _ { \psi \lambda } \\ i _ { \lambda \psi } & i _ { \lambda \lambda } \end{array} \right) .$$

Its inverse is denoted by

$$i \left( \theta \right) ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} { i ^ { \psi \psi } } & { i ^ { \psi \lambda } } \\ { i ^ { \lambda \psi } } & { i ^ { \lambda \lambda } } \end{array} \right)$$

so that, from the formulae for the inverse of a partitioned matrix,

$$\left\{ i ^ { \psi \psi } \right\} ^ { - 1 } = i _ { \psi \psi } - i _ { \psi \lambda } i _ { \lambda \lambda } ^ { - 1 } i _ { \lambda \psi }$$
(A.6)

is the approximate inverse covariance matrix of $\widehat { \psi } .$ Moreover, if $\widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi }$
is the maximum likelihood estimate of $\lambda$ for fixed $\psi ,$ the gradient
vector of the log likelihood, calculated at $\left( \psi , \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } \right) ,$ has approximate
covariance matrix $\left\{ i ^ { \psi \psi } \right\} ^ { - 1 }$ rather than $i _ { \psi \psi }$ . By contrast, the
derivative with respect to $\psi$ at $\left( \psi , \lambda \right)$ has exact covariance matrix
$i _ { \psi \psi } .$ Thus it is reasonable to regard $\left( A . 6 \right)$ rather than $i _ { \psi \psi }$ as the
Fisher information for $\psi$ when $\lambda$ is unknown. Note that two matrix
inversions are required in order to produce the expression in $\left( A . 6 \right) .$
Likelihood-ratio statistics: For large $n$ n, the $\log$ likelihood at $\widehat { \theta }$ differs
from the log likelihood at the true parameter point by a random
amount whose approximate distribution is given by

$$2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } ; Y \right) - 2 l \left( \theta ; Y \right) \sim \chi _ { p } ^ { 2 } + O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) ,$$
(A.7)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="473" -->
<!-- PageHeader="APPENDIX A" -->

where $p$ is the dimension of $\theta$ or the rank of $i \left( \theta \right) .$ If there are
nuisance parameters in the model, the maximized likelihood-ratio
statistic has approximate distribution

$$2 l \left( \widehat { \psi } , \widehat { \lambda } ; Y \right) - 2 l \left( \psi , \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } ; Y \right) \sim \chi _ { p - q } ^ { 2 } + O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) ,$$
(A.8)

where $p \quad - \quad q$ is the dimension of $\psi$ or the rank of $i ^ { \psi \psi }$ in $\left( A . 6 \right) .$
These approximations are often quite accurate for small values of
$n$ even when Normal approximations for parameter estimates are
unsatisfactory. The set of all y-values satisfying

$$2 l \left( \widehat { \psi } , \widehat { \lambda } ; y \right) - 2 l \left( \psi , \widehat { \lambda } _ { \psi } , y \right) \leq \chi _ { p - q , \alpha } ^ { 2 }$$

is an approximate $1 0 0 \left( 1 - \alpha \right) \%$ confidence set for $\psi .$

<!-- PageBreak -->


## APPENDIX B Edgeworth series

Suppose that $Y _ { 1 } , \ldots , Y _ { n }$ are independent and identically distributed
random variables having finite cumulants, $\kappa _ { 1 } \equiv \mu ,$ $\kappa _ { 2 } \equiv \sigma ^ { 2 } ,$ $\kappa _ { 3 } ,$ $\kappa _ { 4 } ,$
up to order four. Define the standardized sum

$$X _ { n } = \frac { Y _ { 1 } + \ldots + Y _ { n } - n \mu } { \sigma \sqrt { n } }$$

Denote by $F _ { n } \left( x \right)$ the probability $\mathrm { p r } \left( X _ { n } \leq x \right) .$ By the central limit
theorem, $X _ { n }$ is asymptotically standard normal and $F _ { n } \left( x \right) \rightarrow \Phi \left( x \right)$
as $n \rightarrow \infty .$ If the distribution of $Y$ has a continuous component,
then $F _ { n } \left( x \right)$ may be approximated more accurately for large $n$ by
an Edgeworth series as follows:

$$E _ { n } \left( x \right) = \Phi \left( x \right) - \phi \left( x \right) \left\{ \rho _ { 3 } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) / \left( 6 n ^ { 1 / 2 } \right) + \rho _ { 4 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - 3 x \right) / \left( 2 4 n \right) \right.$$

$$\left. + \rho _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 5 } - 1 0 x ^ { 3 } + 1 5 x \right) / \left( 7 2 n \right) \right\}$$

where $\rho _ { 3 } = \kappa _ { 3 } / \kappa _ { 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 }$ and $\rho _ { 4 } = \kappa _ { 4 } / \kappa _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ are the standardized cumulants
of $Y .$ The difference $F _ { n } \left( x \right) - E _ { n } \left( x \right)$ is $o \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right)$ uniformly in $x$ on
bounded intervals.

In the case of lattice distributions, this expansion is incorrect
because $F _ { n } \left( x \right)$ is discontinuous with jumps of order $O \left( n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$ at
the possible values of $X _ { n } .$ The Edgeworth approximation is
continuous and hence must involve an error of order $O \left( n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$
near the discontinuities of $F _ { n } \left( x \right) .$ However, the Edgeworth series
can be adjusted to accommodate these discontinuities in $F _ { n } \left( x \right)$ as
follows. Suppose that the possible values of $Y _ { i }$ are the integers
$0 , 1 , 2 , \cdots .$ Define the continuity-corrected abscissa and a 'precision

<!-- PageNumber="474" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="475" -->
<!-- PageHeader="APPENDIX B" -->

adjustment' or Sheppard correction as follows:

$$z = \frac { y _ { 1 } + \ldots + y _ { n } - n \mu + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \sigma \sqrt { n } }$$
$$\tau = 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 4 n \sigma ^ { 2 } }$$
(B.2)

Then the Edgeworth series with the usual two correction terms
may be written

$$F _ { n } \left( z \right) = E _ { n } \left( \tau z \right) + o \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) .$$
(B.3)

This approximation is valid only when computed at the 'continuity-
corrected' points as defined by $\left( B . 2 \right) .$ The distribution function is
constant over intervals of the form $\left[ z - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma \sqrt { n } } \right. ,$ $\left. z + \frac { 1 } { 2 \sigma \sqrt { n } } \right) .$
☒

Note that the correction terms in $\left( B . 3 \right)$ are identical to the
correction terms in (B.1). The only difference is the correction
for continuity and the adjustment of the argument. The continu-
ity correction has an effect of order $O \left( n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$ and the Sheppard
correction has an effect of order $O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) .$

The discrete Edgeworth approximation may be used for the
binomial distribution where $Y \sim B \left( m , \pi \right) ,$ provided that $m$ is
sufficiently large. The relevant coefficients are

$$z = \left( y - m \pi + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) / \sqrt { m \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right) }$$
$$\tau = 1 + 1 / \left\{ 2 4 m \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right) \right\}$$
$$\rho _ { 3 } = \left( 1 - 2 \pi \right) / \sqrt { m \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right) }$$
$$\rho _ { 4 } = \left\{ 1 - 6 \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right) \right\} / \left\{ m \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right) \right\} .$$

The sample size, n, is built into these coefficients through the
binomial index, m. Thus we may take $n = 1$ in $\left( B . 1 \right)$ and $\left( B . 3 \right) .$ In
this case, the approximation seems to be quite accurate if $m \pi \left( 1 - \pi \right)$
exceeds 2.0.

Approximation $\left( B . 3 \right)$ is a simplified version of a series expansion
given by Esseen (1945), who gives the expansion to higher order
than that considered here.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## APPENDIX C Likelihood-ratio statistics

The deviance or deviance difference is just a log-likelihood ratio
statistic. In this appendix, we derive the approximate distribution
of the $\log$ likelihood-ratio statistic for testing a simple null hypoth-
esis concerning a scalar parameter. The corresponding derivations
when there are several parameters of interest or when there are
nuisance parameters follow similar lines but are considerably more
complicated than the proofs presented here.

Suppose that the $\log$ likelihood for $\theta$ based on data $y$ can be
written in the exponential family form

$$l \left( \theta ; y \right) = n \left\{ t \theta - K \left( \theta \right) \right\} ,$$

where $t \equiv t \left( y \right)$ is the sufficient statistic and $\theta$ is called the canonical
parameter. The cumulants of the random variable $T = t \left( Y \right)$ are

$$\kappa _ { r } \left( T \right) = K ^ { \left( r \right) } \left( \theta \right) / n ^ { r - 1 } .$$

The maximum-likelihood estimate of $\theta$ satisfies

$$K ^ { \prime } \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) = t \quad \text { or } \quad \widehat { \theta } = g \left( t \right) , \text { say. } .$$

Hence the $\log$ likelihood-ratio statistic for testing $H _ { 0 } : \theta = \theta _ { 0 }$ is

$$W ^ { 2 } = 2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) - 2 l \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) = 2 n \left\{ t g \left( t \right) - t g \left( \mu _ { 0 } \right) + h \left( t \right) - h \left( \mu _ { 0 } \right) \right\} \quad \left( C . 1 \right)$$

where $h \left( \cdot \right) = K \left( g \left( \cdot \right) \right)$ and $\mu _ { 0 } = K ^ { \prime } \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right)$ is the null mean of $T .$ Thus
$h \left( t \right) = K \left( \widehat { \theta } \right)$ and $h \left( \mu \right) = K \left( \theta \right) .$

Under $H _ { 0 } ,$ the statistic $W ^ { 2 }$ has an approximate $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ distribution
and hence it is reasonable to expect that the signed version

$$W = \pm \left\{ 2 l \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) - 2 l \left( \theta _ { 0 } \right) \right\} ^ { 1 / 2 }$$

<!-- PageNumber="476" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="477" -->
<!-- PageHeader="APPENDIX C" -->

might have an approximate Normal distribution. The sign of $W$
is taken to be the same as that of $t - \mu _ { 0 }$ and, in fact, $W$ is a
monotone increasing function of $t - \mu _ { 0 } .$ To the crudest first-order
of approximation, $W$ is the standardized version of $T ,$ namely

$$X = \left( T - \mu _ { 0 } \right) / \kappa _ { 2 } ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$

If we expand $W$ as a power series in $X$ and keep together terms
that are of the same asymptotic order in $n ,$ we find after a little
effort that

$$W = X - \frac { 1 } { 6 } \rho _ { 3 } X ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 7 2 } \left( 8 \rho _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 3 \rho _ { 4 } \right) X ^ { 3 } + O _ { p } \left( n ^ { - 3 / 2 } \right) ,$$
(C.2)

where $\rho _ { 3 }$ and $\rho _ { 4 }$ are the standardized cumulants of $T$ or the
unstandardized cumulants of $X .$ Note that $\rho _ { 3 } = O \left( n ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right)$ and
$\rho _ { 4 } = O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) .$

It is readily verified that the first two moments of $W$ are

$$E \left( W \right) = \rho _ { 3 } / 6 + O \left( n ^ { - 3 / 2 } \right)$$
$$\mathrm { v a r } \left( W \right) = 1 + \left( 1 4 \rho _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 9 \rho _ { 4 } \right) / 3 6 + O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right)$$

(C.3)

If we define the adjusted statistic or re-standardized statistic

$$W ^ { \prime } = \left\{ W + \frac { 1 } { 6 } \rho _ { 3 } \right\} \left\{ 1 + \left( 9 \rho _ { 4 } - 1 4 \rho _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \right) / 7 2 \right\} ,$$

it is easily checked that, with error $O _ { p } \left( n ^ { - 3 / 2 } \right) ,$

$$W ^ { \prime } = X - \frac { 1 } { 6 } \rho _ { 3 } \left( X ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) - \frac { 1 } { 2 4 } \rho _ { 4 } \left( X ^ { 3 } - 3 X \right) + \frac { 1 } { 3 6 } \rho _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \left( 4 X ^ { 3 } - 7 X \right) .$$

This series can be recognized as the inverse Cornish-Fisher ex-
pansion or the polynomial normalizing transformation. See, for
example, Kendall and Stuart (1977, (6.54)) or McCullagh (1987,
section 5.7 and Exercise 5.15). Provided that $X$ has an Edgeworth
expansion, it follows that

$$W ^ { \prime } \sim N \left( 0 , 1 \right) + O \left( n ^ { - 3 / 2 } \right)$$

in the Edgeworth sense. This conclusion also follows from the
observation that $W ,$ as given in $\left( C . 2 \right) ,$ has third and fourth
cumulants of orders $O \left( n ^ { - 3 / 2 } \right)$ and $O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right)$ respectively.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="478" -->
<!-- PageHeader="LIKELIHOOD-RATIO STATISTICS" -->

In the discrete case, the support points of the distribution of $T$
are usually equally spaced and the discrete Edgeworth approxima-
tion given in Appendix $A$ may be used to approximate the distribu-
tion of $T .$ The support points of $W$ are only approximately equally
spaced. It appears therefore, that the normal approximation with
continuity correction for the distribution of $W ^ { \prime }$ has an error of order
$O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right) .$ The Sheppard correction does not appear to eliminate the
$O \left( n ^ { - 1 } \right)$ error term entirely, though it may reduce it substantially.

As a corollary in the continuous case, it follows directly that

$$W ^ { 2 } \sim \left( 1 + b \right) x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + O \left( n ^ { - 3 / 2 } \right)$$

where $1 + b$ is the sum of the variance and the squared bias of $W$ .
The adjustment

$$b / n = \left( 5 p _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 3 p _ { 4 } \right) / 1 2$$
(C.4)

is known as the Bartlett adjustment factor. Its use greatly improves
the accuracy of the chi-squared approximation for the likelihood-
ratio statistic. In both the discrete and the continuous case, the
cumulants of $W ^ { 2 } / \left( 1 + b \right)$ differ from those of $\chi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ by terms of order
$O \left( n ^ { - 2 } \right) .$

Although the derivations in general are considerably more com-
plicated than that presented here, these results can be extended in
the following ways:

1\. to models not in the exponential family provided that the
usual regularity conditions are satisfied.

2\. to multi-parameter problems.

3\. to problems involving nuisance parameters.

Computational details for generalized linear models are dis-
cussed in section 15.2. For proofs and additional information,
see Lawley (1956), Barndorff-Nielsen and Cox (1984), McCullagh
(1984a, 1987 Chapter 7) and McCullagh and Cox (1986).

<table>
<tr>
<th>* operator, 58</th>

<td>+ operator, 57</td>
